bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Tenderness means being so soft that even touching what is soft becomes intolerable.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
ahaha navāmbuda-kānter amuṣya sukumāratā kumārasya |
api nava-pallava-saṅgād aṅgāny aparajya śīryanti ||341||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Ah! So tender is the body of this young boy with the complexion of a new cloud that by touching new shoots, His limbs become bruised and torn.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
ye nāyaka-prakaraṇe vācikā mānasās tathā |
guṇāḥ proktānta evātra jñeyā uddīpanā budhaḥ ||342||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The intelligent understand that all the verbal and mental qualities which have been mentioned already in the section describing the protagonist are uddīpanas.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
ceṣṭā –
ceṣṭā rāsādi-līlāḥ syus tathā duṣṭa-vadhādayaḥ ||343||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The rasa-līlā, killing of the demons and other pastimes are called activities (ceṣṭā).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra rāso, yathā –
nṛtyad-gopa-nitambinī-kṛta-parīrambhasya rambhādibhir
gīrvāṇībhir anaṅga-raṅga-vivaśaṃ sandṛśyamāna-śriyaḥ |
krīḍā-tāṇḍava-paṇḍitasya paritaḥ śrī-puṇḍarīkākṣa te
rāsārambha-rasārthino madhurimā cetāṃsi naḥ karṣati ||344||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: O lotus-eyed Lord! You, an expert dancer, longing for the pleasure of the rāsa-līlā to begin, were embraced on all sides by gracefully hipped, dancing gopīs. Rambhā and other heavenly damsels, smitten by Cupid’s play, beheld Your beauty at that time. The sweetness exhibited at that time is dragging away our hearts.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
duṣṭa-vadho, yathā lalita-mādhave (9.50) –
śambhur vṛṣaṃ nayati mandara-kandarāntar
mlānaḥ salīlam api yatra śiro dhunāne |
āḥ kautukaṃ kalaya keli-lavād ariṣṭaṃ
taṃ duṣṭa-puṅgavam asau harir unmamātha ||345||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Killing demons, from Lalita-mādhava:

When Ariṣṭāsura shakes his head in jest, Śiva pales and departs for a cave in Mandara Mountain with his bull. Ah! See the fun! Kṛṣṇa so causally has killed that wicked bull demon.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha prasādhanam –
kathitaṃ vasanākalpa-maṇḍanādyaṃ prasādhanam ||346||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Vestment refers to clothing, ornaments, decorations and other things of similar nature.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra vasanam –
navārka-raśmi-kāśmīra-haritālādi-sannibham |
yugaṃ catuṣkaṃ bhūyiṣṭhaṃ vasanaṃ tri-vidhaṃ hareḥ ||347||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The Lord has three types of clothing: two-piece outfit, four-piece dress, and multi-piece dress, in orange, red,[183] yellow, and other colors.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra yugam –
paridhānaṃ sa-saṃvyānaṃ yuga-rūpam udīritam ||348||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Two-piece outfit refers to a lower cloth wrapped around the waist and an upper shawl.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā stavāvalyāṃ mukundāṣṭake (3) –
kanaka-nivaha-śobhā-nindi pītaṃ nitambe
tad-upari nava-raktaṃ vastram itthaṃ dadhānaḥ |
priyam iva kila varṇaṃ rāga-yuktaṃ priyāyāḥ
praṇayatu mama netrābhīṣṭa-pūrtiṃ mukundaḥ ||349||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example from the Mukundāstaka of Stavāvalī:

May Mukunda, wearing on His hips a yellow dhoti which derides the glory of a pile of gold, with reddish upper cloth, tinged with the passion for His beloved, satisfy the desire of my eyes.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
catuṣkam –
catuṣkaṃ kañcukoṣṇīṣa-tunda-bandhāntarīyakam ||350||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The four-piece outfit refers to shirt, turban, sash and lower garment.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
smerāsyaḥ parihita-pāṭalāmbara-śrīś
channāṅgaḥ puraṭa-rucoru-kañcukena |
uṣṇīṣaṃ dadhad aruṇaṃ dhaṭīṃ ca citrām
kaṃsārir vahati mahotsave mudaṃ naḥ ||351||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The enemy of Kaṃsa, smiling in great joy, and wearing a pink dhoti, orange turban, an excellent vest of shining gold, and multi-color sash, creates joy in us.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
bhūyiṣṭham –
khaṇḍitākhaṇḍitaṃ bhūri naṭa-veśa-kriyocitam |
aneka-varṇaṃ vasanaṃ bhūyiṣṭhaṃ kathitaṃ budhaiḥ ||352||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The wise say that the multi-piece outfit consists of many pieces of cloth, cut and uncut, of many colors, suitable for performing artists.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
akhaṇḍita-vikhaṇḍitaiḥ sita-piśaṅga-nīlāruṇaiḥ
paṭaiḥ kṛta-yathocita-prakaṭa-sanniveśojjvalaḥ |
ayaṃ kalabha-rāṭ-prabhaḥ pracura-raṅga-śṛṅgāritaḥ
karoti karabhoru me ghana-rucir mudaṃ mādhavaḥ ||353||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: O slender-thighed woman! Mādhava, the color of the rain cloud, effulgent like a young elephant king, bedecked for a multitude of pastimes, effulgent with a stylish creation of cut and uncut cloth, in white, gold, blue and red colors, is giving me bliss.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha ākalpaḥ –
keśa-bandhanam ālepo mālā-citra-viśeṣakaḥ |
tāmbūla-keli-padmādir ākalpaḥ parikīrtitaḥ ||354||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Hair styles, applied cosmetics, garlands, body tilaka, tilaka on the forehead, betel nut preparations and imitation lotuses are called accessories.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
syāj jūṭaḥ kavarī cūḍā veṇī ca kaca-bandhanam |
pāṇḍuraḥ karburaḥ pīta ity ālepas tridhā mataḥ ||355||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Tying the hair consists of tying the hair at the back of the neck, binding flowers in the hair, binding the hair in a knot at the top, and braiding the hair.[184] The cosmetics are white, multicolor, and yellow.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
mālā tridhā vaijayantī ratna-mālā vana-srajaḥ |
asyā vaikakṣakāpīḍa-prālambādyā bhidā matāḥ ||356||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: There are three types of garland: Vaijayantī, jewel garland and forest garland. They may also extend over the chest, serve as a chaplet around the top knot, or hang from the neck downwards.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
makarī-patra-bhaṅgāḍhyaṃ citraṃ pīta-sitāruṇam |
tathā viśeṣako ’pi syād anyad ūhyaṃ svayaṃ budhaiḥ ||357||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The body tilaka (citram) is yellow, white and reddish, filled with pictures of makarīs[185]. The forehead tilaka (viśeṣaka) is also yellow, white or red. Intelligent persons also modify these into other colors spontaneously.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
tāmbūla-sphurad-ānanendur amalaṃ dhaṃmillam ullāsayan
bhakti-ccheda-lasat-sughṛṣṭa-ghusṛṇālepa-śriyā peśalaḥ |
tuṅgoraḥ-sthala-piṅgala-srag alika-bhrājiṣṇu-patrāṅguliḥ
śyāmāṅga-dyutir adya me sakhi dṛśor dugdhe mudaṃ mādhavaḥ ||358||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: O friend! The moon of His face shines with betel nut and a faultless hair style. A yellow garland rests on His raised chest. He wears radiant tilaka on His forehead, and His body becomes charming with the wealth of designs made of finely ground saffron ointment. Today, the dark-bodied, attractive Mādhava gives bliss to my eyes.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha maṇḍanam –
kirīṭaṃ kuṇḍale hāraś catuṣkī valayormayaḥ |
keyūra-nūpurādyaṃ ca ratna-maṇḍanam ucyate ||359||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The jeweled ornaments (maṇḍanam) include crowns, earrings, necklaces, broaches, bracelets, rings, armbands, and anklets.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
kāñcī citrā mukuṭam atulaṃ kuṇḍale hāri-hīre
hāras tāro valayam amalaṃ candrā-cāruś catuṣkī |
ramyā cormir madhurima-pūre nūpure cety aghārer
aṅgair evābharaṇa-paṭalī bhūṣitā dogdhi bhūṣām ||360||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Colorful belt, an incomparable diadem, earrings of alluring diamonds, a pearl necklace, spotless bracelets, pearl-studded broach, delightful rings, and anklets filled with sweetness—these profuse ornaments attain the status of ornaments because they are decorated by the beauty of the limbs of Kṛṣṇa.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
kusumādi-kṛtaṃ cedaṃ vanya-maṇḍanam īritam |
dhātu-kÿptaṃ tilakaṃ patra-bhaṅga-latādikam ||361||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When these decorations are made of flowers they are called sylvan ornaments. The drawings using curved lines painted on the forehead and body are made of minerals.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha smitaṃ, yathā kṛṣṇa-karṇāmṛte (99) –
akhaṇḍa-nirvāṇa-rasa-pravāhair
vikhaṇḍitāśeṣa-rasāntarāṇi |
ayantritodvānta-sudhārṇavāni
jayanti śītāni tava smitāni ||362||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example of the smile, from Kṛsṇa-karnāmṛta:

O Kṛṣṇa! Your gentle smiles which remove all pain by producing an unbroken stream of rasa of the highest bliss are disgracing all other rasas and emitting an unrestricted ocean of nectar.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha aṅga-saurabhaṃ, yathā –
parimala-sarid eṣā yad vahantī samantāt
pulakayati vapur naḥ kāpy apūrvā munīnām |
madhu-ripur uparāge tad-vinodāya manye
kuru-bhuvam anavadyāmoda-sindhur viveśa ||363||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example of the fragrance of His limbs:

Since the unprecedented river of fragrance flowing everywhere is causing the hairs of us self-satisfied sages to stand on end, I think the enemy of Madhu, a faultless ocean of fragrance, has come to Kurukṣetra to enjoy Himself on the occasion of the eclipse.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha vaṃśaḥ –
dhyānaṃ balāt paramahaṃsa-kulasya bhindan
nindan sudhā-madhurimāṇam adhīra-dharmā |
kandarpa-śāsana-dhurāṃ muhur eṣa śaṃsan
vaṃśī-dhvanir jayati kaṃsa-nisūdanasya ||364||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
His flute:

The fickle sound of Kṛṣṇa’s flute reveals its excellence by breaking the meditation of the exalted sages, criticizing the sweetness of nectar, and insistently preaching respect for the edicts of Cupid.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration: eṣa tridhā bhaved veṇu-muralī-vaṃśikety api ||365||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: There are three types of flutes: the veṇu, the muralī and the vaṃśika.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra veṇuḥ –
pāvikākhyo bhaved veṇur dvādaśāṅguler dairghya-bhāk ||366||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The flute which is twelve fingers long[186] named Pāvika is called veṇu.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
muralī –
hasta-dvayam itāyāmā mukha-randhra-samanvitā |
catuḥ-svara-cchidra-yuktā muralī cāru-nādinā ||367||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The sweet-sounding muralī is two hands long (twenty-four fingers or eighteen inches long) with a hole at the end and four holes for producing sounds.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
vaṃśī –
ardhāṅgulāntaronmānaṃ tārādi-vivarāṣṭakam |
tataḥ sārdhāṅgulād yatra mukha-randhraṃ tathāṅgulam ||368||
śiro vedāṅgulaṃ pucchaṃ try-aṅgulaṃ sā tu vaṃśikā |
nava-randhrā smṛtā sapta-daśāṅgula-mitā budhaiḥ ||369||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The vaṃsikā is seventeen fingers long with nine holes (12.75 inches). Eight holes for playing notes are half a finger in diameter and spaced half a finger apart. A hole for blowing is placed one and a half fingers from the eighth hole and is one finger in diameter. There are four fingers space remaining at the head of the flute and three fingers space remaining at the end of the flute.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
daśāṅgulāntarā syāc cet sā tāra-mukha-randhrayoḥ |
mahānandeti vyākhyātā tathā saṃmohinīti ca ||370||
bhavet sūryāntarā sā cet tata ākarṣiṇī matā |
ānandinī tadā vaṃśī bhaved indrāntarā yadi ||371||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When space between the mouth hole and the first hole for notes is ten fingers, the vaṃśī is called mahānanda (great delight) and sammohinī (the bewitcher). If the space is twelve fingers, the vaṃśi is called ākarṣiṇī (the attractor). If the space is fourteen fingers, the vaṃśī is called ānandinī (bliss-giver).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
gopānāṃ vallabhā seyaṃ vaṃśulīti ca viśrutā |
kramān maṇimayī haimī vaiṇavīti tridhā ca sā ||372||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The ānandinī is a favorite with the cowherd people and is also called vaṃśulī. The vaṃśīs are respectively made of jewels, gold, and bamboo.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha śṛṅgam –
śṛṅgaṃ tu gavalaṃ hema-nibaddhāgrima-paścimam |
ratna-jāla-sphuran-madhyaṃ mandra-ghoṣābhidhaṃ smṛtam ||373||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
The horn:

A wild buffalo horn with gold covering at both ends and jewel inlay in the middle is called Mandraghoṣa (Rumbling Thunder).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
tārāvalī veṇu-bhujaṅgamena
tārāvalīlā-garalena daṣṭā |
viṣāṇikā-nāda-payo nipīya
viṣāṇi kāmaṃ dvi-guṇī-cakāra ||374||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Bitten by the flute, like a scorpion with natural poisonous venom in the form of its shrill sound, the gopī Tārāvalī, leader of a group, drank some milk in the form of the sound of a small buffalo horn in order to counteract the poison. But rather than mitigate the burning poison of the flute’s sound, it increased the pain twofold.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha nūpuraṃ, yathā –
agha-mardanasya sakhi nūpura-dhvaniṃ
niśamayya sambhṛta-gabhīra-sambhramā |
aham īkṣaṇottaralitāpi nābhavaṃ
bahir adya hanta guravaḥ puraḥ sthitāḥ ||375||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example of His anklets:

Having heard the sound of Kṛṣṇa’s ankle bells, I have become extremely impatient to see Him, and am possessed with intense zeal. But I cannot leave the place because my elders are present in front of me.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha kambuḥ –
kambus tu dakṣiṇāvartaḥ pāñcajanyatayocyate ||376||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
The conch:

The conch with its spiral turning to the right is called Pāñcajanya.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
amara-ripu-vadhūṭī-bhrūṇa-hatyā-vilāsī
tridiva-pura-purandhrī-vṛnda-nāndīkaro ’yam |
bhramati bhuvana-madhye mādhavādhmāta-dhāmnaḥ
kṛta-pulaka-kadambaḥ kambu-rājasya nādaḥ ||377||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The sound of the king of conches, Pāñcajanya, blown by Kṛṣṇa, wanders throughout the world, causing abortions in the wives of the demons, announcing auspiciousness to the inhabitants of Svarga, and making the hair stand on end.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha padāṅkaḥ, yathā śrī-daśame –
tad-darśanāhlāda-vivṛddha-sambhramaḥ
premṇordhva-romāśru-kalākulekṣaṇaḥ |
rathād avaskandya sa teṣv aceṣṭata
prabhor amūny aṅghri-rajāṃsy aho iti ||378||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example of His footprints, from the Tenth Canto:

Increasingly agitated by ecstasy at seeing the Lord’s footprints, his bodily hairs standing on end because of his pure love, and his eyes filled with tears, Akrūra jumped down from his chariot and began rolling about among those footprints, exclaiming, “Ah, this is the dust from my master’s feet!” SB 10.38.26

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
kalayata harir adhvanā sakhāyaḥ
sphuṭam amunā yamunā-taṭīm ayāsīt |
harati pada-tatir yad-akṣiṇī me
dhvaja-kuliśākuśa-paṅkajāṅkiteyam ||379||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

O friends! Understand that Kṛṣṇa has gone to the bank of the Yamunā by this path for certain, because the markings of the flag, thunderbolt, goad and lotus are attracting my eyes.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha kṣetram, yathā –
hari-keli-bhuvāṃ vilokanaṃ
bata dūre ’stu sudurlabha-śriyām |
mathurety api karṇa-paddhatiṃ
praviśan nāma mano dhinoti naḥ ||380||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example of His place:

What to speak of seeing all the places of rare glory where the Lord performed pastimes, even hearing the name of Mathurā steals away my mind.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha tulasī, yathā bilvamaṅgale[188] –
ayi paṅkaja-netra-mauli-māle
tulasī-mañjari kiñcid arthayāmi |
avabodhaya pārtha-sārathes tvaṃ
caraṇābja-śaraṇābhilāṣiṇaṃ mām ||381||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example of tulasī, from Kṛṣṇa-karṇāmṛta:

O tulasī bud from lotus-eyed Kṛṣṇa’s chaplet! I make one prayer to you. Inform the chariot driver of Arjuna that I desire the shelter of His lotus feet.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha bhakto, yathā caturthe –
vijñāya tāv uttama-gāya-kiṅkarāv
abhyutthitaḥ sādhvasa-vismṛta-kramaḥ |
nanāma nāmāni gṛṇan madhu-dviṣaḥ
pārṣat-pradhānāv iti saṃhatāñjaliḥ ||382||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example of the devotee as uddīpana, from the Fourth Canto:

Dhruva Mahārāja, seeing that these uncommon personalities were direct servants of the Supreme Personality of Godhead, immediately stood up. But, being puzzled, in hastiness he forgot how to receive them in the proper way. Therefore, he simply offered obeisances with folded hands and chanted and glorified the holy names of the Lord. SB 4.12.21

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
subala bhuja-bhujaṅgaṃ nyasya tuṅge tavāṃse
smita-vilasad-apāṅgaḥ prāṅgaṇe bhrājamānaḥ |
nayana-yugam asiñcad yaḥ sudhā-vīcibhir naḥ
kathaya sa dayitas te kvāyam āste vayasyaḥ ||383||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

O Subala! Please tell us where your dear friend Kṛṣṇa is. While throwing side glances endowed with a gentle smile and placing His arm on your raised shoulder while stand in the yard, He would inundate our eyes with waves of nectar.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha tad-vāsaro, yathā –
adbhutā bahavaḥ santu bhagavat-parva-vāsarāḥ |
āmodayati māṃ dhanyā kṛṣṇa-bhādrapadāṣṭamī ||384||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example of the days commemorating the Lord:

here are many astonishing festival days celebrating the Lord. However, the auspicious eighth tithi of the waning moon in the month of Bhadra (Janmāṣṭami) gives me great joy.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
anubhāvās tu citta-stha-bhāvānām avabodhakāḥ |
te bahir vikriyā prāyāḥ proktā udbhāsvarākhyayā ||1||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Anubhāvas refers to those things which express the bhāva situated within the heart. They are predominantly external transformations, and are thus called udbhāsvara (shining in the body).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
nṛtyaṃ viluṭhitaṃ gītaṃ krośanaṃ tanu-moṭanam |
huṅkāro jṛmbhaṇaṃ śvāsa-bhūmā lokānapekṣitā |
lālā-sravo ’ṭṭahāsaś ca ghūrṇā-hikkādayo ’pi ca ||2||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: External transformations known as anubhāvas are actions, such as dancing, rolling on the ground, singing, shouting, stretching the body, bellowing, yawning, breathing heavily, disregarding others, drooling, laughing loudly, whirling around, and hiccups.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
te śītāḥ kṣepaṇāś ceti yathārthākhyā dvidhoditāḥ |
śītāḥ syur gīta-jṛmbhādyā nṛtyādyāḥ kṣepaṇābhidhāḥ ||3||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Anubhāvas are of two types with the suitable names of śīta (meaning “cool,” with lack of bodily movement) and kṣepaṇa (meaning “throwing about,” involving distinct bodily movements). Śīta includes singing, yawning, breathing heavily, disregarding others, drooling and smiling. Kṣepaṇa includes dancing, rolling on the ground, shouting, stretching the body, bellowing, laughing loudly,[189] whirling around and having hiccups.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra nṛtyaṃ, yathā –
muralī-khuralī-sudhā-kiraṃ
hari-vaktrendum avekṣya kampitaḥ |
gaṇane sagaṇeśa-ḍiṇḍima-
dhvanibhis tāṇḍavam āśrito haraḥ ||4||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Dancing:

Seeing the moon-like face of Hari, radiating sweetness as He practiced His flute playing, Lord Śiva began to tremble and then began to dance in the sky along with Ganeśa to the rhythm of his drum.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
viluṭhitaṃ, yathā tṛtīye –
kaccid budhaḥ svasty-anamīva āste
śvaphalka-putro bhagavat-prapannaḥ |
yaḥ kṛṣṇa-pādāṅkita-mārga-pāṃsuṣv
aceṣṭata prema-vibhinna-dhairyaḥ ||5||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Falling on the ground, from the Third Canto:

Please tell me whether Akrūra, the son of Śvaphalka, is doing well. He is a faultless soul surrendered unto the Personality of Godhead. He once lost his mental equilibrium due to his ecstasy of transcendental love and fell down on the dust of a road which was marked with the footprints of Lord Kṛṣṇa. SB 3.1.32

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
navānurāgeṇa tavāvaśāṅgī
vana-srag-āmodam avāpya mattā |
vrajāṅgane sā kaṭhine luṭhantī
gātraṃ sugātrī vraṇayāñcakāra ||6||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

O Kṛṣṇa! Because of her new attraction for You, Rādhā is not in control of her limbs. On smelling the fragrance of Your forest garland, becoming intoxicated, she has fallen on the hard ground of Vraja and injured her beautiful body.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
gītaṃ, yathā –
rāga-ḍambara-karambita-cetāḥ
kurvatī tava navaṃ guṇa-gānam |
gokulendra kurute jalatāṃ sā
rādhikādya dṛṣadāṃ suhṛdāṃ ca ||7||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Singing:

Moon of Gokula! Today, Rādhā, whose mind is possessed by the beauty of the śrīrāga (or the zeal of love), is singing a new song about you. Her friends have become like stones, and the stones have melted.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
krośanaṃ, yathā –
hari-kīrtana-jāta-vikriyaḥ
sa vicukrośa tathādya nāradaḥ |
acirān nara-siṃha-śaṅkayā
danujā yena dhṛtā vililyire ||8||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Shouting:

Because of the transformations brought about by the chanting of the name of Hari, Nārada began shouting in such a manner that the demons immediately hid themselves, trembling in fear that Narasiṃha had made His appearance.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
urarīkṛta-kākur ākulā
kurarīva vraja-rāja-nanda |
muralī-taralī-kṛtāntarā
muhur ākrośad ihādya sundarī ||9||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

O son of Vraja’s king! Today in Vṛndāvana the beautiful Rādhā, her heart melted by sound of Your flute, was constantly shouting in agitation like a female osprey with a changed voice.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tanu-mocanaṃ, yathā –
kṛṣṇa-nāmani mudopavīṇite
prīṇite manasi vaiṇiko muniḥ |
udbhaṭaṃ kim api moṭayan
vapus troṭayaty akhila-yajña-sūtrakam ||10||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Stretching the body:

Nārada, blissfully singing the names of Kṛṣṇa on his vīnā with a happy mind, stretched his body in a most astonishing way and completely broke his sacred thread.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
huṅkāro, yathā –
vaiṇava-dhvanibhir udbhramad-dhiyaḥ
śaṅkarasya divi huṅkṛti-svanaḥ |
dhvaṃsayann api muhuḥ sa-dānavaṃ
sādhu-vṛndam akarot sadā navam ||11||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Bellowing:

Hearing the sound of the vīṇā, Śiva, mind excited, bellowed in such a way that it destroyed the demons and gave the highest bliss to the devotees at every moment.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
jṛmbhaṇam, yathā –
vistṛta-kumuda-vane ’sminn
udayati pūrṇe kalānidhau purataḥ |
tava padmini mukha-padmaṃ
bhajate jṛmbhām aho citram ||12||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Yawning:

O lotus![190] How astonishing that in the grove of blossoming water lilies, you have blossomed at the rising of the full moon on the eastern horizon.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
śvāsa-bhūmā, yathā –
upasthite citra-paṭāmbudāgame
vivṛddha-tṛṣṇā lalitākhya-cātakī |
niḥśvāsa-jhañjhā-marutāpavāhitaṃ
kṛṣṇāmbudākāram avekṣya cukṣubhe ||13||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Heavy breathing:

When the monsoon arrived with colorful clothing, the Cātakī bird named Lalitā became increasingly thirsty. Seeing the form of that black cloud called Kṛṣṇa driven away by the strong breathing of the monsoon wind mixed with rain, she became disturbed.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
lokānapekṣitā, yathā śrī-daśame –
aho paśyata nārīṇām api kṛṣṇe jagad-gurau |
duranta-bhāvaṃ yo ’vidhyan mṛtyupāśān gṛhābhidhān ||14||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Disregard for all others, from the Tenth Canto:

Just see the unlimited love these women have developed for Lord Kṛṣṇa, the spiritual master of the entire universe! This love has broken their very bonds of death—their attachment to family life. SB 10.23.41

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā padyāvalyām (73) –
parivadatu jano yathā tathā vā
nanu mukharo na vayaṃ vicārayāmaḥ |
hari-rasa-madirā madātimattā
bhuvi viluṭhāma naṭāma nirviśāma ||15||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example, from Padyāvalī:

Let the big mouths gossip! We will not consider it at all! By tasting the sweet liquor of Hari, we have become joyful and intoxicated. Let us fall on the ground, dance and enjoy.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
lālā-sravo, yathā –
śaṅke prema-bhujaṅgena daṣṭaḥ kaṣṭaṃ gato muniḥ |
niścalasya yad etasya lālā sravati vaktrataḥ ||16||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Drooling:

I think that Nārada, bitten by the scorpion of prema, is suffering in pain, because he has fallen into a motionless state, and saliva is dripping from his mouth.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
aṭṭahāsaḥ –
hāsād bhinno ’ṭṭahāso ’yaṃ citta-vikṣepa-sambhavaḥ ||17||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Laughing loudly:

That laughing which arises from disturbance of the heart is called laughing loudly or aṭṭahāsa. This is distinguished from the normal laugh.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
śaṅke ciraṃ keśava-kiṅkarasya
cetas taṭe bhakti-latā praphullā |
yenādhi-tuṇḍa-sthalam aṭṭahāsa-
prasūna-puñjāś caṭulaṃ skhalanti ||18||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

I think that the creeper of devotion in the heart of the servant of Kṛṣṇa has been blossoming for a long time, because a multitude of flowers in the form of his loud laughing is shaking his trembling lips.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
ghūrṇā, yathā –
dhruvam agharipur ādadhāti vātyāṃ
nanu murali tvayi phutkṛti-cchalena |
kim ayam itarathā dhvanir vighūrṇanaṃ
sakhi tava ghūrṇayati vrajāmbujākṣīḥ ||19||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Whirling around:

O my friend, dear flute! Kṛṣṇa, on the pretext of blowing you, is certainly producing a gale. Otherwise, how does your sound alone cause the beautiful women of Vraja to whirl around?

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
hikkā, yathā –
na putri racayauṣadhaṃ visṛja rodam atyuddhataṃ
mudhā priya-sakhīṃ prati tvam aśivaṃ kim āśaṅkase |
hari-praṇaya-vikriyākulatayā bruvāṇā muhur
varākṣi harir ity asau vitanute ’dya hikkā-bharam ||20||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Hiccups:

O daughter! Are you worrying uselessly that something has happened to your dear friend Rādhā? Do not apply any medicine! Give up this loud weeping! O beautiful eyed girl! Today, Rādhā, afflicted by the transformations brought on by love for Kṛṣṇa, is afflicted with hiccups while chanting His name incessantly.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
vapur utphullatāraktodgamādyāḥ syuḥ pare ’pi ye |
atīva-viralatvāt te naivātra parikīrtitāḥ ||21||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Other symptoms such as extreme goose bumps and sweating blood are not described here since they are very rare.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
kṛṣna-sambandhibhiḥ sākṣāt kiñcid vā vyavadhānataḥ |
bhāvaiś cittam ihākrāntaṃ sattvam ity ucyate budhaiḥ ||1||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: On the topic of rasa, when the heart becomes overwhelmed by ratis or bhāvas in relation to Kṛṣṇa, either directly or indirectly, the learned call this sattva.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
sattvād asmāt samutpannā ye ye bhāvās te tu sāttvikāḥ |
snigdhā digdhās tathā rukṣā ity amī trividhā matāḥ ||2||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The transformations which arise solely from this sattva are called sāttvika-bhāvas. There are three types of sāttvika-bhāvas: snigdha (affectionate, arising from genuine rati), digdha (tainted, arising from other emotions) and rukṣa (contaminated, arising in a person without rati).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra snigdhāḥ –
snigdhās tu sāttvikā mukhyā gauṇāś ceti dvidhā matāḥ ||3||

tatra mukhyāḥ –
ākramān mukhyayā ratyā mukhyāḥ syuḥ sāttvikā amī |
vijñeyaḥ kṛṣṇa-sambandhaḥ sākṣād evātra sūribhiḥ ||4||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Principal snigdha-sāttvika-bhāva:

The principal sāttvika-bhāvas arise from a principal rati. The wise call this relationship with Kṛṣṇa direct.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
kundair mukundāya mudā sṛjantī
srajāṃ varāṃ kunda-viḍambi-dantī |
babhūva gāndharva-rasena veṇor
gāndharvikā spandana-śūnya-gātrī ||5||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example of principal snigdha- sāttvika-bhāva:

Rādhā, whose teeth were whiter than the kunda flower, while making an excellent garland of kunda flowers for Mukunda, heard the sweet song of the flute. She became stunned.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration: mukhyaḥ stambha ’yam itthaṃ te jñeyāḥ svedādayo ’pi ca ||6||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: In this example, being stunned is a principal snigdha-sāttvika-bhāva (since it arises from a principal rati, madhura-rati). In the same way, the appearance of other sāttvika-bhāvas such as perspiring should be understood.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha gauṇāḥ –
ratyākramaṇataḥ proktā gauṇās te gauṇa-bhūtayā |
atra kṛṣṇasya sambandhaḥ syāt kiñcid vyavadhānataḥ ||7||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Secondary snigdha-sāttvika-bhāva.

Sāttvika-bhāvas which arise from a secondary rati are called secondary snigdha-sāttvika-bhāvas. The relationship with Kṛṣṇa is somewhat indirect.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
sva-vilocana-cātakāmbude
puri nīte puruṣottame purā |
atitāmra-mukhī sagadgadaṃ
nṛpam ākrośati gokuleśvarī |
imau gauṇau vaivarṇya-svara-bhedau ||8||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

When Kṛṣṇa, the rain cloud for the cātaka birds of her eyes, was brought to Mathurā, Yaśodā, turning red-faced in anger, began to scold Nanda Mahārāja with a choked voice. In this example, the change of color and choked voice of Yaśodā have been caused by the secondary rati of anger (krodha-rati).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha digdhāḥ –
rati-dvaya-vinābhūtair bhāvair manasa ākramāt |
jane jāta-ratau digdhās te ced raty-anugāminaḥ ||9||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Digdha-sāttvika-bhāva:

When the heart of a person who possesses genuine rati is overcome by an emotion other than the primary or secondary rati and if this emotion appears along with a genuine rati, it is called digdha-sāttvika-bhāva.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
pūtanām iha niśāmya niśāyāṃ
sā niśānta-luṭhad-udbhaṭa-gātrīm |
kampitāṅga-latikā vraja-rājñī
putram ākula-matir vicinoti ||10||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

One time, Yaśodā, while dreaming at night, saw the huge body of Pūtanā rolling on the ground in her house. Her body began shaking. Then, and in great agitation, she began to search for Kṛṣṇa.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration: kampo raty-anugāmitvād asau digdha itīryate ||11||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Since this shaking of her body accompanies the actual rati for Kṛṣṇa, it is called digdha.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
rukṣāḥ –
madhurāścarya-tad-vārtotpannair mud-vismayādibhiḥ |
jātā bhaktopame rukṣā rati-śūnye jane kvacit ||12||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Rukṣa-sāttvika-bhāva.

Sometimes there are apparent sāttvika-bhāvas similar to those of persons possessing rati, appearing in persons without real rati, through astonishment or bliss caused by hearing about the sweet and astonishing Lord. This is called rukṣa-sāttvika-bhāva.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
bhogaika-sādhana-juṣā rati-gandha-śūnyaṃ
svaṃ ceṣṭayā hṛdayam atra vivṛṇvato ’pi |
ullāsinaḥ sapadi mādhava-keli-gītais
tasyāṅgam utpulakitaṃ madhurais tadāsīt ||13||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

A person who is revealed to be devoid of rati by absorbing himself in the pursuit of material enjoyment may develop hairs standing on end after being excited by songs concerning the pastimes of Mādhava.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
rukṣa eṣa romāñcāḥ –
rukṣo ’yaṃ rati-śūnyatvād romāñcaṃ kathito budhaiḥ |
mumukṣu-prabhṛtau pūrvaṃ yo ratābhyāsa īritaḥ ||14||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The standing of hairs on end which arises without real rati being present in the person is an example of rukṣa-sāttvika-bhāva. Raty-abhāsa which appears in persons desiring liberation, as described previously (1.3.44), gives rise to rukṣa-sāttvika-bhāvas. [194]

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
cittaṃ sattvībhavat prāṇe nyasyaty ātmānam udbhaṭam |
prāṇas tu vikriyāṃ gacchan dehaṃ vikṣobhayaty alam |
tadā stambhādayo bhāvā bhakta-dehe bhavanty amī ||15||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When the heart becomes overwhelmed with bhāva related to Kṛṣṇa, it offers itself to the prāṇa with force. The prāṇa undergoes change, and disturbs the body. Then the sāttvika-bhāvas such as paralysis become visible in the devotee’s body.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
te stambha-sveda-romāñcāḥ svara-bhedo ’tha vepathuḥ |
vaivarṇyam aśru pralaya ity aṣṭau sāttvikāḥ smṛtāḥ ||16||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The eight sāttvika bhāvas are paralysis, perspiration, hairs standing on end, choking of the voice, trembling, changing color, tears, and fainting.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
catvāri kṣmādi-bhūtāni prāṇo jātv avalambate |
kadācit sva-pradhānaḥ san dehe carati sarvataḥ ||17||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The prāṇa takes shelter of the four elements earth, water, fire and ether, and sometimes takes shelter of itself. The prāṇa then moves throughout the body.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
stambhaṃ bhūmi-sthitaḥ prāṇas tanoty aśru-jalāśrayaḥ |
tejasthaḥ sveda-vaivarṇye pralayaṃ viyad-āśrayaḥ ||18||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When the prāṇa takes shelter of the earth element, paralysis arises. When the prāṇa takes shelter of the water element, tears arise. When the prāṇa takes shelter of the fire element, perspiration and change of color arise. When the prāṇa takes shelter of the ether element, fainting arises.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
svastha eva kramān manda-madhya-tīvratva-bheda-bhāk |
romāñca-kampa-vaisvarṇyāṇy atra trīṇi tanoty asau ||19||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When the prāṇa takes shelter of itself to a small degree the hairs stand on end. When the prāṇa takes shelter of itself to a moderate degree, the body shakes. When the prāṇa takes shelter of itself to the extreme, the voice chokes up.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
bahir antaś ca vikṣobha-vidhāyitvād ataḥ sphuṭam |
proktānubhāvatāmīṣāṃ bhāvatā ca manīṣibhiḥ ||20||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Because of this, the sāttvika-bhāvas produce intense disturbance both externally and internally. The wise call the disturbance to the body (external), the anubhāva aspect of the sāttvika-bhāva, and the disturbance to the heart (internal), the vyabhicāri aspect of the sāttvika-bhāva.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra stambhaḥ –
stambho harṣa-bhayāścarya-viṣādāmarṣa-sambhavaḥ |
tatra vāg-ādi-rāhityaṃ naiścalyaṃ śūnyatādayaḥ ||21||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Paralysis:

Paralysis arises from joy, fear, astonishment, disappointment and indignation.[195] There is immobility of the action senses and knowledge gathering senses.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra harṣād, yathā tṛtīye–
yasyānurāga-pluta-hāsa-rāsa-
līlāvaloka-pratilabdha-mānāḥ |
vraja-striyo dṛgbhir anupravṛtta-
dhiyo ’vatasthuḥ kila kṛtya-śeṣāḥ ||22||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Paralysis arising from joy, from the Third Canto:

The damsels of Vraja, after pastimes of laughter, humor and exchanges of glances, used to follow Him with their eyes, and thus they sat down with stunned intelligence and could not finish their household duties. SB 3.2.14

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
bhayād, yathā –
giri-sannibha-malla-cakra-ruddhaṃ
purataḥ prāṇa-parārdhataḥ parārdhyam |
tanayaṃ jananī samīkṣya śuṣyan
nayanā hanta babhūva niścalāṅgī ||23||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From fear:

When Devakī saw her son Kṛṣṇa, dearer than billions of her lives, being attacked by the wrestlers, her eyes become dry and she became paralyzed.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
āścaryād, yathā śrī-daśame–
tato ’tikutukodvṛtya-stimitaikādaśendriyaḥ |
tad-dhāmnābhūd ajas tūṣṇīṃ pūr-devy-antīva putrikā ||24||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From astonishment, from the Tenth Canto:

Then, by the power of the effulgence of those Viṣṇu forms, Lord Brahmā, his eleven senses jolted by astonishment and stunned by transcendental bliss, became silent, just like a child’s clay doll in the presence of the village deity. SB 10.13.56

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
śiśoḥ śyāmasya paśyantī śailam abhraṃlihaṃ kare |
tatra citrārpitevāsīd goṣṭhī goṣṭha-nivāsinām ||25||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example of stambha arising from astonishment:

Seeing that Govardhana Mountain, touching the sky, was held up by the hand of the small child, the inhabitants of Vraja became immobile like figures in a painting.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
viṣādād, yathā –
baka-sodara-dānavodare
pūrataḥ prekṣya viśantam acyutam |
diviṣan-nikaro viṣaṇṇa-dhīḥ
prakaṭaṃ citrapaṭāyate divi ||26||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From sorrow:

Seeing before them that Kṛṣṇa was entering the stomach of Aghāsura, who was the brother of Bakāsura, the devatās in the sky, overcome with sorrow, became clearly like painted pictures.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
amarṣād, yathā –
kartum icchati mura-dviṣe puraḥ
patri-mokṣam akṛpe kṛpī-sute |
satvaro ’pi ripu-niṣkraye ruṣā
niṣkriyaḥ kṣaṇam abhūt kapi-dhvajaḥ ||27||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From indignation:

When merciless Aśvatthāma became eager to shoot arrows at Kṛṣṇa, Arjuna, though hasty to respond to his enemy, became motionless for some time because of his anger.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha svedaḥ –
svedo harṣa-bhaya-krodhādi-jaḥ kleda-karas tanoḥ ||28||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Perspiration:

Perspiration arises from joy, fear and anger. It makes the body damp.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra harṣād, yathā –
kim atra sūryātapam ākṣipantī
mugdhākṣi cāturyam urīkaroṣi |
jñātaṃ puraḥ prekṣya saroruhākṣaṃ
svinnāsi bhinnā kusumāyudhena ||29||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From Joy:

O Rādhā with joyful eyes! Why are you deriding the heat of the sun and emanating such an amiable nature? I understand that you have been pierced by the arrows of love, for you are perspiring on seeing lotus-eyed Kṛṣṇa in front of you.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
bhayād, yathā –
kutukād abhimanyu-veṣiṇaṃ
harim ākruśya girā pragalbhayā |
viditākṛtir ākulaḥ kṣaṇād
ajani svinna-tanuḥ sa raktakaḥ ||30||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From Fear:

Kṛṣṇa one time put on the dress of Abhimanyu for fun. His servant, Raktaka, thinking that He was Abhimanyu, called out to Him using impudent words. After understanding that He was actually Kṛṣṇa, he became every frightened and for some time became covered with perspiration.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
krodhād, yathā –
yajñasya bhaṅgād ativṛṣṭi-kāriṇaṃ
samīkṣya śakraṃ saruṣo garutmataḥ |
ghanopariṣṭād api tiṣṭhatas tadā
nipetur aṅgād ghana-nīra-bindavaḥ ||31||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From anger:

Seeing Indra showering down excessive rain because his sacrifice had been stopped, Garuḍa, though situated on a cloud at a distance, became filled with anger. Profuse drops of perspiration fell from his limbs.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha romāñcaḥ –
romāñco ’yaṃ kilāścarya-harṣotsāha-bhayādijaḥ |
romāṇām abhyudgamas tatra gātra-saṃsparśanādayaḥ ||32||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Standing of hairs on end:

Standing of hairs on end takes place from astonishment, joy, enthusiasm and fear. In this state, all the hairs of the body stand on end, which sends a thrill throughout the body.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra āścaryād, yathā –
ḍimbhasya jṛmbhāṃ bhajatas trīlokīṃ
vilokya vailakṣyavatī mukhāntaḥ |
babhūva goṣṭhendra-kuṭumbinīyaṃ
tanu-ruhaiḥ kuḍmalitāṅga-yaṣṭiḥ ||33||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From astonishment:

Yaśodā became astonished at seeing the heavenly, middle and lower planetary systems within the mouth of Kṛṣṇa when He was beginning to crawl about. The creeper of her body began to blossom with hairs standing on end.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
harṣād, yathā śrī-daśame–
kiṃ te kṛtaṃ kṣiti tapo bata keśavāṅghri-
sparśotsavotpulakitāṅga-ruhair vibhāsi |
apy aṅghri-sambhava urukrama-vikramād vā
āho varāha-vapuṣaḥ parirambhaṇena ||34||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From Joy from the Tenth Canto:

O mother earth, what austerity did you perform to attain the touch of Lord Keśava’s lotus feet, which has brought you such great joy that your bodily hairs are standing on end? You appear very beautiful in this condition. Was it during the Lord’s current appearance that you acquired this ecstatic symptom, or was it perhaps much earlier, when He stepped upon you in His form of the dwarf Vāmanadeva, or even earlier, when He embraced you in His form of the boar Varāhadeva? SB 10.30.10

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
utsāhād, yathā –
śṛṅgaṃ kelir aṇārambhe raṇayaty agha-mardane |
śrīdāmno yoddhu-kāmasya reme romāñcitaṃ vapuḥ ||35||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From eagerness:

When Kṛṣṇa sounded His horn during a mock battle, Śrīdāma became eager to fight and his body hairs stood on end.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
bhayād, yathā –
viśva-rūpa-dharam adbhutākṛtiṃ
prekṣya tatra puruṣottamaṃ puraḥ |
arjunaḥ sapadi śuṣyad-ānanaḥ
śiśriye vikaṭa-kaṇṭakāṃ tanum ||36||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From fear:

When Arjuna saw before him the Supreme Lord Kṛṣṇa as the astonishing universal form, his face dried up, and the hairs of his body suddenly stood on end.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha svara-bhedaḥ –
visāda-vismayāmarṣa-harṣa-bhīty-ādi-sambhavam |
vaisvaryaṃ svara-bhedaḥ syād eṣa gadgadikādikṛt ||37||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Choking of the voice:

Distortion of the voice is called svara-bheda. It arises from sorrow, astonishment, anger, joy and fear. It causes convulsions in speaking.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra viṣādād , yathā –
vraja-rājñi rathāt puro hariṃ
svayam ity ardha-viśīrṇa-jalpayā |
hriyam eṇadṛśā gurāv api
ślathayantyā kila roditā sakhī ||38||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From lamentation:

“O queen of Vraja, Yaśodā! Please personally take Kṛṣṇa from the chariot in front of you (embarking for Mathurā).” Doe-eyed Rādhā has made her friends weep by giving up bashfulness in front of elders and uttering half of these words in a choked voice.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
vismayād, yathā śrī-daśame–
śanair athotthāya vimṛjya locane
mukundam udvīkṣya vinamra-kandharaḥ |
kṛtāñjaliḥ praśrayavān samāhitaḥ
sa-vepathur gadgadayailatelayā ||39||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From astonishment, from the Tenth Canto:

Then, rising very gradually and wiping his two eyes, Lord Brahmā looked up at Mukunda. Lord Brahmā, his head bent low, his mind concentrated and his body trembling, very humbly began, with faltering words, to offer praises to Lord Kṛṣṇa. SB 10.13.64

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
amarṣād, yathā tatraiva–
preṣṭhaṃ priyetaram iva pratibhāṣamāṇaṃ
kṛṣṇaṃ tad-artha-vinivartita-sarva-kāmāḥ |
netre vimṛjya ruditopahate sma kiñcit
saṃrambha-gadgada-giro ’bruvatānuraktāḥ ||40||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From anger, also from the Tenth Canto:

Although Kṛṣṇa was their beloved, and although they had abandoned all other objects of desire for His sake, He had been speaking to them unfavorably. Nonetheless, they remained unflinching in their attachment to Him. Stopping their crying, they wiped their eyes and began to speak, their voices stammering with agitation. SB 10.29.30

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
harṣād, yathā tatraiva–
hṛṣyat-tanūruho bhāva-pariklinnātma-locanaḥ ||
girā gadgadayāstauṣīt sattvam ālambya sātvataḥ |
praṇamya mūrdhnāvahitaḥ kṛtāñjali-puṭaḥ śanaiḥ ||41||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From joy, also from the Tenth Canto:

As the great devotee Akrūra beheld all this, he became extremely pleased and felt enthused with transcendental devotion. His intense ecstasy caused His bodily hairs to stand on end and tears to flow from his eyes, drenching his entire body. Somehow managing to steady himself, Akrūra bowed his head to the ground. Then he joined his palms in supplication and, in a voice choked with emotion, very slowly and attentively began to pray. SB 10.39.56-57

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
bhīter, yathā –
tvayyārpītaṃ vitara venum iti pramādī
śrutvā mad īratam udīrṇa vivarṇa bhāvaḥ |
tūrṇaṃ babhūva guru-gadgada-ruddha-kaṇṭhaḥ
patrī mukunda tad anena sa harito ’sti. ||42||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From Fear:

I said to your servant Patrī, “Give me that flute kept with you.” Hearing my words, the careless Patrī suddenly changed color, and his voice choked up. O Mukunda! Because of his inattention, Your flute has been lost.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha vepathuḥ –
vitrāsāmarṣa-harṣādyair vepathur gātra-laulya-kṛt ||43||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Trembling:

Quivering of the limbs (gātra-laulya-kṛt) due to extreme fear, anger or joy is called vepathu or trembling.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra vitrāsena, yathā –
śaṅkha-cūḍam adhirūḍha-vikramaṃ
prekṣya vistṛta-bhujaṃ jighṛkṣayā |
hā vrajendra-tanayeti-vādinī
kampa-sampadam adhatta rādhikā ||44||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From fear:

When Śaṅkhacūḍa, with increasing display of his prowess, stretched out his hand to grab Rādhā, she cried out, “O son of the king of Vraja!” Because of fear, her whole body began to shake.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
amarṣeṇa, yathā –
kṛṣṇādhikṣepa-jātena vyākulo nakulāmbujaḥ |
cakampe drāg amarṣeṇa bhū-kampe girirāḍ iva ||45||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From anger:

Becoming unsteady with anger on hearing criticism of Kṛṣṇa (by Śiśupāla), Sahadeva began to shake like a great mountain during an earthquake.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
harṣeṇa, yathā –
vihasasi kathaṃ hatāśe paśya bhayenādya kampamānāsmi |
cañcalam upasīdantaṃ nivāraya vraja-pates tanayam ||46||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From joy:

O foolish friend! Why are you smiling? See--I am now trembling with fear. Keep away the fickle son of Nanda who is approaching.[196]

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha vaivarṇyam –
viṣāda-roṣa-bhīty-āder vaivarṇyaṃ varṇa-vikriyā |
bhāva-jñair atra mālinya-kārśyādyāḥ parikīrtitāḥ ||47||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Changing of color:

Change of a person’s complexion (varṇa-vikriyā) due to grief, anger[197] or fear is called vaivarṇya or change of color.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra viṣādād, yathā –
śvetīkṛtākhila-janaṃ viraheṇa tavādhunā |
gokulaṃ kṛṣṇa devarṣeḥ śvetadvīpa-bhramaṃ dadhe ||48||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From grief:

O Kṛṣṇa! In separation from You, all the inhabitants of Vraja have now turned white, such that Nārada has mistaken Gokula for Svetadvīpa.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
roṣād, yathā –
kaṃsa-śatrum abhiyuñjataḥ puro
vīkṣya kaṃsa-sahajānudāyudhān |
śrī-balasya sakhi tasya ruṣyataḥ
prodyad-indu-nibham ānanaṃ babhau ||49||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From anger:

O friend! See how the face of angry Balarāma is glowing red like the newly risen moon on seeing in front of Him the assistants of Kaṃsa with weapons in hand ready to fight with Kṛṣṇa.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
bhīter, yathā –
rakṣite vraja-kule bakāriṇā
parvataṃ varam udasya līlayā |
kālimā bala-ripor mukhe bhavann
ūcivān manasi bhītim utthitām ||50||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From fear:

When the inhabitants of Vraja were protected by Kṛṣṇa, the enemy of Baka, as He lifted the huge mountain casually, the face of Indra turned black. This indicated the fear in his mind.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
viṣāde śvetimā proktā dhausaryaṃ kālimā kvacit |
roṣe tu raktimā bhītyāṃ kālimā kvāpi śuklimā ||51||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: It is said that the change of color from grief is white and sometimes grey or black. Change of color from anger is red. From fear, the color change is black and sometimes white.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
raktimā lakṣyate vyakto harṣodreke ’pi kutracit |
atrāsārvatrikatvena naivāsyodāhṛtiḥ kṛtā ||52||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When it arises from joy the color is sometimes red, and but since this is not universal, examples of turning red from joy are not given.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha aśru –
harṣa-roṣa-viṣādādyair aśru netre jalodgamaḥ |
harṣaje ’śruṇi śītatvam auṣṇyaṃ roṣādi-sambhave |
sarvatra nayana-kṣobha-rāga-saṃmārjanādayaḥ ||53||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Tears:

Where water flows from the eyes (jalodgamaḥ) out of joy, anger or grief it is called tears (aśru). Tears generated out of joy are cool, and tears generated out of anger are warm. In all cases, there is unsteady movement of the eyes, redness of the eyes and rubbing the eyes.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atra harṣeṇa, yathā –
govinda-prekṣaṇākṣepi-bāṣpa-pūrābhivarṣiṇam |
uccair anindad ānandam aravinda-vilocanā ||54||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From joy:

The lotus-eyed Rukmiṇī derided bliss because the flow of tears arising from the bliss blocked her vision of Govinda.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
roṣeṇa, yathā hari-vaṃśe (2.66.24) –
tasyāḥ susrāva netrābhyāṃ vāri praṇaya-kopajam |
kuśeśaya-palāśābhyām avaśyāya-jalaṃ yathā ||55||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From anger, from Hari-vaṃśa:

From the lotus-petal eyes of Satyabhāmā fell tears generated by angry affection, like drops of dew.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
bhīmasya cedīśa-vadhaṃ vidhitso
reje ’śru-visrāvi ruṣoparaktam |
udyan mukhaṃ vāri-kaṇāvakīrṇaṃ
sāndhya-tviṣā grastam ivendu-bimbam ||56||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

When Bhīma desired to kill Śiśupāla, his face turned red and became covered with tears of anger. It appeared like the rising full moon covered with drops of water and tinged with red in the dawn.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
viṣādena, yathā śrī-daśame–
padā sujātena nakhāruṇa-śriyā
bhuvaṃ likhanty aśrubhir añjanāsitaiḥ |
āsiñcatī kuṅkuma-rūṣitau stanau
tasthāv adho-mukhy atiduḥkha-ruddha-vāk ||57||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From disappointment, from the Tenth Canto:

With her tender foot, effulgent with the reddish glow of her nails, she scratched the ground, and tears darkened by her eye makeup sprinkled her kuṃkuma-reddened breasts. There she stood, face downward, her voice choked up by extreme sorrow. SB 10.60.23

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha pralayaḥ –
pralayaḥ sukha-duḥkhābhyāṃ ceṣṭā-jñāna-nirākṛtiḥ |
atrānubhāvāḥ kathitā mahī-nipatanādayaḥ ||58||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Pralaya:

Pralaya or fainting refers to absence of action of the body and absence of mental functions which distinguish self and objects. This arises from either happiness or distress. It is characterized by falling on the ground etc.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra sukhena, yathā –
milantaṃ harim ālokya latā-puñjād atarkitam |
jñapti-śūnya-manā reje niścalāṅgī vrajāṅganā ||59||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From happiness:

When Kṛṣṇa suddenly appeared out of the tangle of creepers, the gopīs, seeing that they were again united with Him, became motionless and devoid of external consciousness.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
duḥkhena, yathā śrī-daśame –
anyāś ca tad-anudhyāna-nivṛttāśeṣa-vṛttayaḥ |
nābhyajānan imaṃ lokam ātma-lokaṃ gatā iva ||60||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From distress, from the Tenth Canto:

Other gopīs entirely stopped their sensory activities and became fixed in meditation on Kṛṣṇa. They lost all awareness of the external world, just like those who attain the platform of self-realization. SB 10.39.15

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
sarve hi sattva-mūlatvād bhāvā yadyapi sāttvikāḥ |
tathāpy amīṣāṃ sattvaika-mūlatvāt sāttvika-prathā ||61||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: All the anubhāvas could be called sāttvika, since their root is a transformation of the mind arising from rati.[199] However, since the eight states just mentioned, beginning with stambha, arise solely from transformations arising from rati, they are called sāttvika-bhāvas.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
sattvasya tāratamyāt prāṇa-tanu-kṣobha-tāratamyaṃ syāt |
tata eva tāratamyaṃ sarveṣāṃ sāttvikānāṃ syāt ||62||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Because of the variations in sattva (disturbance of the mind due to rati), there is variation in the disturbance of the life airs and body. In other words, there are various degrees of all the sāttvika-bhāvas.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
dhūmāyitās te jvalitā dīptā uddīpta-saṃjñitāḥ |
vṛddhiṃ yathottaraṃ yāntaḥ sāttvikāḥ syuś catur-vidhāḥ ||63||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The sāttvika-bhāvas are of four types when they attain increasing degrees of intensity: dhūmāyita (smoky), jvalita (luminous); dīpta (brilliant); uddīpta (very brilliant).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
sā bhūri-kāla-vyāpitvaṃ bahv-aṅga-vyāpitā ’pi ca |
svarūpeṇa tathotkarṣa iti vṛddhis tridhā bhavet ||64||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The increase is of three types: duration of the symptoms, pervasion of the symptoms in different parts of the body, and exhibition of the essential nature of the sāttvika-bhāva.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra netrāmbu-vaisvarya-varjānām eva yujyate |
bahv-aṅga-vyāpitāmīṣāṃ tayoḥ kāpi viśiṣṭatā ||65||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Except for tears and choking of the voice, the other sāttvika-bhāvas can spread through many parts of body. Tears and choking of the voice have some special features.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatrāśrūṇāṃ dṛg-aucchūnya-kāritvam avadātatā |
tathā tārātivaicitrī-vailakṣaṇya-vidhāyitā |
vaisvarṇyasya tu bhinnatve kauṇṭhya-vyākulatādayaḥ ||66||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The special features of tears are swelling of the eyes and whiteness of the eyes. The pupils of the eyes become extremely colorful. The special features of choking of the voice are breaking of the voice, weakness of the voice and fluctuation of tone.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
bhinnatvaṃ sthāna-vibhraṃśaḥ kauṇṭhyaṃ syāt sanna-kaṇṭhatā |
vyākulatvaṃ tu nānocca-nīca-gupta-viluptatā ||67||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Breaking of the voice means failure of the vocal cords to pronounce properly. Weakness means inability to make any sound. Fluctuation means high, low, indistinct and inaudible tones.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
prāyo dhūmāyitā eva rukṣās tiṣṭhanti sāttvikāḥ |
snigdhās tu prāyaśaḥ sarve caturdhaiva bhavanty amī ||68||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: All the sāttvika-bhāvas in the rukṣa state (in persons without real rati) remain generally at the dhūmāyita level. The sāttvika-bhāvas in the snigdha state appear in all the four levels (dhūmāyita, jvalita, dīpta and uddīpta).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
mahotsavādi-vṛtteṣu sad-goṣṭhī-tāṇḍavādiṣu |
jvalanty ullāsinaḥ kvāpi te rukṣā api kasyacit ||69||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Sometimes, however, the rukṣa-sāttvika-bhāvas of a person who is enthusiastic to dance or perform other devotional acts amongst the devotees on the occasion of festivals reaches the jvalita stage.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
sarvānanda-camatkāra-hetur bhāvo varo ratiḥ |
ete hi tad-vinā-bhāvān na camatkāritāśrayāḥ ||70||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Rati is the cause of all types of bliss. Therefore, rati is called the most excellent bhāva. Being devoid of this rati, rukṣa or other types of bhāva can never be the shelter of bliss.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra dhūmāyitāḥ –
advitīyā amī bhāvā athavā sa-dvitīyakāḥ |
īṣad-vyaktā apahnotuṃ śakyā dhūmāyitā matāḥ ||71||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Smoky sāttvika-bhāva:

Any sāttvika-bhāva which appears alone or with others, which manifests slightly and can be hidden by the individual, is called dhūmāyita-sāttvika-bhāva.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
ākarṇayann aghaharām agha-vairi-kīrtiṃ
pakṣmāgra-miśra-viralāśrur abhūt purodhāḥ |
yaṣṭā darocchvasita-loma-kapolam īṣat-
prasvinna-nāsikam uvāha mukhāravindam ||72||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

When the performer of sacrifice heard the glories of Kṛṣṇa killing Aghāsura, the tips of his eyes filled with a few tears, hairs stood up on his cheeks and a few drops of perspiration appears on his nose. In this way his lotus face shone.[202]

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha jvalitāḥ –
te dvau trayo vā yugapad yāntaḥ suprakaṭāṃ daśām |
śakyāḥ kṛcchreṇa nihnotuṃ jvalitā iti kīrtitāḥ ||73||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Luminous sāttvika-bhāva:

When two or three of the sāttvika-bhāvas appear very clearly, and can be concealed only with difficulty, they are called jvalita-sāttvika-bhāvas.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
na guñjām ādātuṃ prabhavati karaḥ kampa-taralo
dṛśau sāsre piñchaṃ na paricinutaḥ satvara-kṛti |
kṣamāv ūrū stabdhau padam api na gantuṃ tava sakhe
vanād vaṃśī-dhvāne parisaram avāpte śravaṇayoḥ ||74||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

One sakhī said to Kṛṣṇa, “When the sound of Your flute comes from the forest and arrives at my ears, my hands begin to shake, and I cannot quickly pluck the guñja berries. My eyes become filled with tears, and I cannot immediately recognize the peacock feather. My two thighs become paralyzed and I cannot easily walk one step.”

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
niruddhaṃ bāṣpāmbhaḥ katham api mayā gadgada-giro
hriyā sadyo gūḍhāḥ sakhi vighaṭito vepathur api |
giri-droṇyāṃ veṇau dhvanati nipuṇair iṅgita-maye
tathāpy ūhāñcakre mama manasi rāgaḥ parijanaiḥ ||75||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

O friend! When the sound of the flute indicating the presence of Kṛṣṇa manifests in the ravine, I stop the flow of tears, I hide my choked voice and conceal the trembling of my body. However, skilful persons have guessed that I have attraction for Kṛṣṇa in my heart.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha dīptāḥ –
prauḍhāṃ tri-caturā vyaktiṃ pañca vā yugapad-gatāḥ |
saṃvarītum aśakyās te dīptā dhīrair udāhṛtāḥ ||76||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Brilliant sāttvika-bhāva:

When three, four or five sāttvika-bhāvas appear strongly and cannot be concealed, they are called dīpta-sāttvika-bhāvas.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
na śaktim upavīṇane ciram adhatta kampākulo
na gadgada-niruddha-vāk prabhur abhūd upaślokane |
kṣamo ’jani na vīkṣaṇe vigalad-aśru-puraḥ puro
madhu-dviṣi parisphuraty avaśam-mūrtir āsīn muniḥ ||77||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

When Nārada saw Kṛṣṇa appear before him, he lost control of his body. Due to trembling of his body, for a long time he was unable to play his vīṇā, and due to choked voice, he was unable to recite verses of praise. Because his eyes were filled with tears, he could not see Kṛṣṇa.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
kim unmīlaty asre kusumaja-rajo gañjasi mudhā
sa-romāñce kampe himam anilam ākrośasi kutaḥ |
kim ūru-stambhe vā vana-viharaṇaṃ dvekṣi sakhi te
nirābādhā rādhe vadati madanādhiṃ svara-bhidā ||78||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

O Rādhā, my friend! Because tears have come to your eyes, why are you uselessly scolding the flower pollen? Because your hairs are standing on end and your body is quivering, why are you uselessly scolding the cool wind? Because your limbs have become paralyzed, why are you uselessly showing anger about walking in the forest? Your choked voice which you cannot hide reveals the pain of love.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha uddīptāḥ –
ekadā vyaktim āpannāḥ pañca-ṣāḥ sarva eva vā |
ārūḍhā paramotkarṣam uddīptā iti kīrtitāḥ ||79||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Very brilliant sāttvika-bhāva:

When five, six or all the sāttvika-bhāvas manifest at once, in their most extreme form, they are called uddīpta-sāttvika-bhāvas.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
adya svidyati vepate pulakibhir nispandatām aṅgakair
dhatte kākubhir ākulaṃ vilapati mlāyaty analpoṣmabhiḥ |
stimyaty ambubhir ambaka-stabakitaiḥ pītāmbaroḍḍāmaraṃ
sadyas tad-viraheṇa muhyati muhur goṣṭhādhivāsī janaḥ ||80||

uddīptā eva sūddiptā mahā-bhāve bhavanty amī |
sarva eva parāṃ koṭiṃ sāttvikā yatra bibhrati ||81||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When all the uddīpta-sāttvika-bhāvas appear in mahā-bhāva they are called sūddīpta-sāttvika-bhāvas. All the sāttvika-bhāvas attain their highest state in mahā-bhāva.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
kiṃ ca –
athātra sāttvikābhāsā vilikhyante catur-vidhāḥ ||82||
raty-ābhāsa-bhavās te tu sattvābhāsa-bhavās tathā |
niḥsattvāś ca pratīpāś ca yathā-pūrvam amī varāḥ ||83||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: However, four types of ābhāsas of sāttvika-bhāva should be described. They are called ratyābhāsa-bhava (generated from raty-ābhāsa), sattvābhāsa-bhava (generated from sattvābhāsa), niḥsattva (false sattva) and pratīpa (enmity). They are listed in order from superior to inferior.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra ādyāḥ –
mumukṣu-pramukheṣv ādyā raty-ābhāsāt puroditāt ||84||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Ratyābhāsa-bhava-sattvikābhāsa, symptoms which appear to be sāttvika-bhāvas, generated from the ratyābhāsa described previously, arises in people desiring liberation.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
vārāṇasī-nivāsī kaścid ayaṃ vyāharan hareś caritam |
yati-goṣṭhyām utpulakaḥ siñcati gaṇḍa-dvayīm asraiḥ ||85||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

When a person living in Vārānasī glorified the qualities of Hari repeatedly in the assembly of sannyāsīs, his hairs stood on end and tears moistened his cheeks.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha sattvābhāsa-bhavāḥ –
mud-vismayāder ābhāsaḥ prodyan jātyā ślathe hṛdi |
sattvābhāsa iti proktaḥ sattvābhāsa-bhavās tataḥ ||86||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When a shadow (ābhāsa) of joy, astonishment or other emotions appears in the heart of a person who is soft by nature (sentimental), the heart is said to have developed a state of sattvābhāsa. From this state of sattvābhāsa arise symptoms similar to sāttvika-bhāvas. They are called sattvābhāsa-bhava.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
jaran-mīmāṃsakasyāpi śṛṇvataḥ kṛṣṇa-vibhramam |
hṛṣṭāyamāna-manaso babhūvotpulakaṃ vapuḥ ||87||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

When an aged person expert in Mīmāṃsa scriptures heard the pastimes of Kṛṣṇa, he became joyful in heart and his hairs stood on end.[204]

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
mukunda-caritāmṛta-prasara-varṣiṇas te mayā
kathaṃ kathana-cāturī-madhurimā gurur varṇyatām |
muhūrtam atad-arthino ’pi viṣayiṇov ’pi yasyānanān
niśamya vijayaṃ prabhor dadhati bāṣpa-dhārām amī ||88||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

How can I describe the sweetness of your skilful words pouring a stream of nectar with descriptions of the pastimes of Mukunda? When the materialists who do not even desire to hear about Mukunda hear the pastimes of Mukunda from your mouth, their eyes quickly become filled with tears.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha niḥsattvāḥ –
nisarga-picchila-svānte tad-abhyāsa-pare ’pi ca |
sattvābhāsaṃ vināpi syuḥ kvāpy aśru-pulakādayaḥ ||89||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When a person has a hard heart and practices exhibiting the sāttvika-bhāvas, without even a touch of emotion, the appearance of tears or other symptoms are called niḥsattva.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
niśamayato hari-caritaṃ na hi sukha-duḥkhādayo ’sya hṛdi bhāvāḥ|
anabhiniveśāj jātā katham asravad asram aśrāntam ||90||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

When a person, though hearing the pastimes of the Lord, does not feel either happiness or distress because of hardness of the heart, how can tears constantly flow from his eyes? It must be from practice alone that the tears flow.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
prakṛtyā śithilaṃ yeṣāṃ manaḥ picchilam eva vā |
teṣv eva sāttvikābhāsaḥ prāyaḥ saṃsadi jāyate ||91||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Those whose minds are either soft or hard generally show sāttvikābhasa only in festive gatherings of chanting the names of the Lord.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha pratīpāḥ –
hitād anyasya kṛṣṇasya pratīpāḥ krud-bhayādibhiḥ ||92||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Sāttvikābhāsa within the enemies of Kṛṣṇa generated from anger or fear is called pratīpa-sāttvikābhāsa.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra krudhā, yathā hari-vaṃśe (2.30.63) –
tasya prasphuritauṣṭhasya raktādhara-taṭasya ca |
vaktraṃ kaṃsasya roṣeṇa rakta-sūryāyate tadā ||93||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From anger, from Hari-vaṃśa:

With red lower lip and trembling upper lip, Kaṃsa’s face appeared like the sun, red with anger.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
bhayena, yathā –
mlānānanaḥ kṛṣṇam avekṣya raṅge
siṣveda mallas tv adhi-bhāla-śukti |
mukti-śriyāṃ suṣṭhu puro milantyām
atyādarāt pādyam ivājahāra ||94||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From fear:

Seeing Kṛṣṇa in the arena the wrestler’s face turned pale, and drops of perspiration appeared on his forehead. It seemed that his forehead like a conch shell was offering arghya with great reverence to the goddess of liberation who had just arrived in front of him.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
pravācyamāne purataḥ purāṇe
niśamya kaṃsasya bhayātirekam |
pariplavāntaḥkaraṇaḥ samantāt
parimlāna-mukhas tadāsīt ||95||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example of fear:

Hearing of Kaṃsa’s extreme fear during a recital of the Purāṇas, one person (identifying with Kaṃsa’s fear) began to tremble within and his face darkened.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
nāsty arthaḥ sāttvikābhāsa-kathane ko ’pi yadyapi |
sāttvikānāṃ vivekāya dik tathāpi pradarśitā ||96||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Though there is no necessity of describing sāttvikābhāsa, a sketch has been given in order to become familiar with all aspects of sāttvika-bhāvas.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
athocyante trayas-triṃśad-bhāvā ye vyabhicāriṇaḥ |
viśeṣeṇābhimukhyena caranti sthāyinaṃ prati ||1||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Hereafter, the thirty-three vyabhicāri-bhāvas will be described. They are called vyabhicāri-bhāvas since they move (caranti) against the sthāyi-bhāva, while assisting it in a distinctive way (viśeṣena abhimukhyena)

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
vāg-aṅga-sattva-sūcyā jñeyās te vyabhicāriṇaḥ |
sañcārayanti bhāvasya gatiṃ sañcāriṇo ’pi ||2||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The vyabhicāri-bhāvas reveal themselves by words, by eye brows and other bodily parts and by external actions (anubhāvas) which arise from overwhelming emotions (sattva).[205] Since they set in motion (sañcārayanti) the course of the sthāyi-bhāva, they are called sañcāri-bhāvas.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
unmajjanti nimajjanti sthāyiny amṛta-vāridhau |
ūrmivad vardhayanty enaṃ yānti tad-rūpatāṃ ca te ||3||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: All the vyabhicāri-bhāvas, rising and falling like waves in the sweet ocean of the sthāyi-bhāva, increase the sthāyi-bhāva and then merge into the sthāyi-bhāva.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
nirvedo ’tha viṣādo dainyaṃ glāni-śramau ca mada-garvau |
śaṅkā-trāsāvegā unmādāpasmṛtī tathā vyādhiḥ ||4||
moho mṛtir ālasyaṃ jāḍyaṃ vrīḍāvahitthā ca |
smṛtir atha vitarka-cintā-mati-dhṛtayo harṣa utsukatvaṃ ca ||5||
augryāmarṣāsūyāś cāpalyaṃ caiva nidrā ca |
suptir bodha itīme bhāvā vyabhicāriṇaḥ samākhyātāḥ ||6||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The vyabhicāri-bhāvas are as follows: self-disgust (nirveda), remorse (viṣāda), thinking oneself unqualified (dainyam or dīnatā), debility (glāni or mlāni), fatigue (śrama), rapture (mada), pride (garva), apprehension (śaṅkā), sudden fear (trāsa), confusion of the mind (āvega), insanity (unmāda), epilepsy (apasmṛti), sickness (vyādhi), loss of internal awareness (moha), death-like symptoms (mṛti), sloth (ālasyam), indecision (jāḍyam), shame (vrīḍā), concealment (avahitthā), remembrance (smṛti), conjecture (vitarka), pondering (cintā), finding meaning through scriptural reference (mati), steadiness (dhṛti), joy (harṣa), impatience (autsukyam), ferocity (augrya), indignation (amarṣa), fault-finding (asūyā), insolence (cāpalya), sleep (nidrā), dreaming (supti), and enlightenment (bodha).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra (1) nirvedaḥ –
mahārti-viprayogerṣyā-sad-vivekādi-kalpitam |
svāvamānanam evātra nirveda iti kathyate |
atra cintāśru-vaivarṇya-dainya-niḥśvasitādayaḥ ||7||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Self-disgust arising from great sorrow, separation, hatred or worrying about doing what should not be done, or not doing what should be done, is called nirveda. In this state worry, tears, change of color, feeling of lack of qualities (dainyam) and sighing occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra mahārtyā, yathā –
hanta deha-hatakaiḥ kim amībhiḥ
pālitair viphala-puṇya-phalair naḥ |
ehi kāliya-hrade viṣa-vahnau
svaṃ kuṭumbini haṭhāj juhavāma ||8||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From great sorrow:

O Yaśodā! What is to be gained from continuing to maintain this sinful, unfortunate body? Come! We will immediately offer our bodies in the lake of Kāliya filled with the fire of poison.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
viprayogeṇa, yathā –
asaṅgamān mādhava-mādhurīṇām
apuṣpite nīrasatāṃ prayāte |
vṛndāvane śīryati hā kuto ’sau
prāṇity apuṇyaḥ subalo dvirephaḥ ||9||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From separation:

Without the presence of sweetness of Mādhava, Vṛndāvana became withered, without charm and devoid of flowers. How does this unfortunate, strong bee continue to live?

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā, dāna-keli-kaumudyām (20) –
bhavatu mādhava-jalpam aśṛṇvatoḥ
śravaṇayor alam aśravaṇir mama |
tam avilokayator avilocaniḥ
sakhi vilocanayoś ca kilānayoḥ ||10||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From Dāna-keli-kaumudī:

O friend! Without hearing the words of Mādhava, my ears may as well become deaf. Without seeing the form of Mādhava, my eyes may as well become blind.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
īrṣyayā, yathā hari-vaṃśe (2.67.11) satyādevī-vākyam –
stotavyā yadi tāvat sā nāradena tavāgrataḥ |
durbhago ’yaṃ janas tatra kim artham anuśabditaḥ ||11||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From anger, in the words of Satyabhāmā, Hari-vaṃśa:

O Kṛṣṇa! If Nārada is praising Rukmiṇī in front of You, he is calling upon her a misfortune similar to mine.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
sad-vivekena, yathā śrī-daśame–
mamaiṣa kālo ’jita niṣphalo gato
rājya-śriyonnaddha-madasya bhūpateḥ |
martyātma-buddheḥ suta-dāra-koṣa-bhūṣv
āsajjamānasya duranta-cintayā ||12||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Through discrimination, from the Tenth Canto:

I have wasted all this time, O unconquerable one, becoming more and more intoxicated by my domain and opulence as an earthly king. Misidentifying the mortal body as the self, becoming attached to children, wives, treasury and land, I suffered endless anxiety. SB 10.51.47

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
amaṅgalam api procya nirvedaṃ prathamaṃ muniḥ |
mene ’muṃ sthāyinaṃ śānta iti jalpanti kecana ||13||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Though it is inauspicious, Bharata Muni has mentioned nirveda as the first vyabhicāri-bhāva, since it is the sthāyi-bhāva for śānta-rasa. This is the opinion of some persons.[207]

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (2) viṣādaḥ –
iṣṭānavāpti-prārabdha-kāryāsiddhi-vipattitaḥ |
aparādhādito ’pi syād anutāpo viṣaṇṇatā ||14||
atropāya-sahāyānusandhiś cintā ca rodanam |
vilāpa-śvāsa-vaivarṇya-mukha-śoṣādayo ’pi ca ||15||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Remorse:

Remorse or despair arising from failure to attain one’s desired object, from failure to accomplish a task, from occurrence of a disaster, or from committing an offense is called viṣāda. In this state, there is worry, search for a means of accomplishing, search for assistance, weeping, moaning, heavy breathing, change of color and drying of the mouth.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra iṣṭānavāptito, yathā –
jarāṃ yātā mūrtir mama vivaśatāṃ vāg api gatā
mano-vṛttiś ceyaṃ smṛti-vidhuratā-paddhatim agāt |
agha-dhvaṃsin dūre vasatu bhavad-ālokana-śaśī
mayā hanta prāpto na bhajana-rucer apy avasaraḥ ||16||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From not attaining one’s desired object:

O Kṛṣṇa, killer of the Agha demon! My body is afflicted with age, my words are uncontrolled and my mind is without memory power. What to speak of attaining the moon of bliss on seeing You, I have not even attained the opportunity of desiring to worship You!

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
prārabdha-kāryāsiddheḥ, yathā –
svapne mayādya kusumāni kilāhṛtāni
yatnena tair viracitā vana-mālikā ca |
yāvan mukunda-hṛdi hanta nidhīyate sā
hā tāvad eva tarasā virarāma nidrā ||17||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From failure to accomplish an action:

Today in a dream I was picking flowers and very carefully made a garland out of them. But just when I thought of offering it to the heart of Mukunda, my sleep broke.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
vipattiteḥ, yathā –
katham anāyi pure mayakā sutaḥ
katham asau na nigṛhya gṛhe dhṛtaḥ |
amum aho bata danti-vidhuntudo
vidhuritaṃ vidhum atra vidhitsati ||18||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Remorse arising from impending disaster:

I am so unfortunate! Why did I take my son to Mathurā? Why did I not forcibly keep Him in my house? In Mathurā the elephant desires to afflict my son just as Rāhu desires to afflict the moon.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
aparādhāt, yathā śrī-daśame –
paśyeśa me ’nāryam ananta ādye
parātmani tvayy api māyi-māyini
māyāṃ vitatyekṣitum ātma-vaibhavaṃ
hy ahaṃ kiyān aiccham ivārcir agnau ||19||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Remorse arising from committing an offense, from the Tenth Canto:

My Lord, just see my uncivilized impudence! To test Your power, I tried to extend my illusory potency to cover You, the unlimited and primeval Supersoul, who bewilder even the masters of illusion. What am I compared to You? I am just like a small spark in the presence of a great fire. SB 10.14.9

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
syamantakam ahaṃ hṛtvā gato ghorāsyam antakam |
karavai taraṇīṃ kāṃ vā kṣipto vaitaraṇīyam anu ||20||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example or remorse arising from committing offense:

Having stolen the Syamantaka jewel, I have fallen into the mouth of terrible hell. Having fallen in the Vaitaraṇī River, what boat should I use to cross over it?

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (3) dainyam –
duḥkha-trāsāparādhādyair anaurjityaṃ tu dīnatā |
cāṭu-kṛn-māndya-mālinya-cintāṅga-jaḍimādi-kṛt ||21||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Thinking oneself a low creature because of sorrow, fear or offense is called dainyam or dīnatā. In this state there are words of flattery, feebleness of the heart, impurity of the heart, thinking various thoughts, and immobility of the limbs.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra duḥkhena, yathā śrī-daśame –
ciram iha vṛjinārtas tapyamāno ’nutāpair
avitṛṣa-ṣaḍa-mitro labdha-śāntiḥ kathaṅcit |
śaraṇada samupetas tvat-padābjaṃ parātmann
abhayam ṛtam aśokaṃ pāhi māpannam īśa ||22||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Humility arising from sorrow, from the Tenth Canto:

For so long I have been pained by troubles in this world and have been burning with lamentation. My six enemies are never satiated, and I can find no peace. Therefore, O giver of shelter, O Supreme Soul, please protect me! O Lord, in the midst of danger I have by good fortune approached Your lotus feet, which are the truth and which thus make one fearless and free of sorrow. SB 10.51.57

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
trāsena, yathā prathame –
abhidravati mām īśa śaras taptāyaso vibho |
kāmaṃ dahatu māṃ nātha mā me garbho nipātyatām ||23||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Lowness arising from fear, from the First Canto:

O my Lord, You are all-powerful. A fiery iron arrow is coming towards me fast. My Lord, let it burn me personally, if You so desire, but please do not let it burn and abort my embryo. Please do me this favor, my Lord. SB 1.8.10

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
aparādhena, yathā śrī-daśame –
ataḥ kṣamasvācyuta me rajo-bhuvo
hy ajānatas tvat-pṛthagīśa-māninaḥ
ajāvalepāndhatamo ’ndhacakṣuṣa
eṣo ’nukampyo mayi nāthavān iti ||24||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Lowness arising from committing an offense, from the Tenth Canto:

Therefore, O infallible Lord, kindly excuse my offenses. I have taken birth in the mode of passion and am therefore simply foolish, presuming myself a controller independent of Your Lordship. My eyes are blinded by the darkness of ignorance, which causes me to think of myself as the unborn creator of the universe. But please consider that I am Your servant and therefore worthy of Your compassion. SB 10.14.10

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
ādya-śabdena lajjayāpi, yathā tatraiva
mā ’nayaṃ bhoḥ kṛthās tvāṃ tu nanda-gopa-sutaṃ priyam |
jānīmo ’ṅga vraja-ślāghyaṃ dehi vāsāṃsi vepitāḥ ||25||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
The word adya in duḥkha-trāsāparādhādyair (verse 21) indicates that dainyam also arises from shame. This is illustrated in the Bhāgavatam:

Dear Kṛṣṇa, don’t be unfair! We know that You are the respectable son of Nanda and that You are honored by everyone in Vraja. You are also very dear to us. Please give us back our clothes. We are shivering in the cold water. SB 10.22.14

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (4) mlāniḥ –
ojaḥ somātmakaṃ dehe bala-puṣṭi-kṛd asya tu |
kṣayāccham ādhi-raty-ādyair glānir niṣprāṇatā matā |
kampāṅga-jāḍya-vaivarṇya-kārśya-dṛg-bhramaṇādi-kṛt ||26||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Ojas, whose ruling deity is the moon, produces strength and nourishment in the body. When it decreases by physical exertion, mental anxiety or the sexual act, the weakened state is called glāni or mlāni. In the state of glāni or languishing there is trembling, indecision, change of color, becoming thin and throwing glances here and there.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra śrameṇa, yathā –
āghūrṇan-maṇi-valayojjvala-prakoṣṭhā
goṣṭhāntar-madhuripu-kīrti-nartitauṣṭhī |
lolākṣī dadhi-kalasaṃ viloḍayantī
kṛṣṇāya klama-bhara-niḥspṛhā babhūva ||27||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Glāni arising from physical exertion:

One time Rādhā was churning yogurt for Kṛṣṇa. At that time here jewel studded bracelet on her hand began to shake. Her lips began to sing the glories of Kṛṣṇa, living in Vraja. In fear of her elders, her eyes began to move about. Churning the yoghurt in this way, she became extremely tired, and could not move her limbs.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
gumphituṃ nirupamāṃ vana-srajaṃ
