cāru puṣpa-paṭalaṃ vicinvatī |
durgame klama-bharātidurbalā
kānane kṣaṇam abhūn mṛgekṣaṇā ||28||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

In order to string an incomparable garland for Kṛṣṇa, doe-eyed Rādhā went into an inaccessible forest. While picking the beautiful flowers, she became very weak for a few moments, due to exhaustion.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
ādhinā, yathā –
sā rasa-vyatikareṇa vihīnā
kṣīṇa-jīvana-taroccala-haṃsā |
mādhavādya viraheṇa tavāmbā
śuṣyati sma sarasī śucineva ||29||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Fatigue due to mental anxieties:

Because of the summer heat, the lake’s water dries up and becomes devoid of lotuses and water birds. Mādhava! In a similar way, in separation, Your mother Yaśodā, devoid of happiness, has become weakened. Her soul has departed and her body is withering away.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
ratyā, yathā rasa-sudhākare (2.13f) –
ati-prayatnena ratānta-tāntā
kṛṣṇena talpāvaropitā sā |
ālambya tasyaiva karaṃ kareṇa
jyotsnā-kṛtānandam alindam āpa ||30||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Fatigue arising from amorous actions, from Rasa-sudhākara:

At the conclusion of amorous activities, Kṛṣṇa raised Rādhā very carefully from the bed. Rādhā then held His hand and came to the veranda of the house shining with moonlight.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (5) śramaḥ –
adhva-nṛtya-ratādy-utthaḥ khedaḥ śrama itīryate |
nidrā-svedāṅga-saṃmarda-jṛmbhāśvāsādi-bhāg asau ||31||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Fatigue arising from losing the way, dancing or amorous activities is called śrama. In this state, sleep, perspiration, rubbing the body, yawning and heavy breathing appear.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha adhvano, yathā –
kṛtāgasaṃ putram anuvrajantī
vrajājirāntar vraja-rāja-rājñī |
pariskhalat-kuntala-bandhaneyaṃ
babhūva gharmāmbu-karambitāṅgī ||32||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Fatigue from losing the way:

When Kṛṣṇa offended his mother and fled away, she pursued her son in Vraja. Her hair become unbound and she began to perspire.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
nṛtyādeḥ, yathā –
vistīryottaralita-hāram aṅga-hāraṃ
saṅgītonmukha-mukharair vṛtaḥ suhṛdbhiḥ |
asvidyad viracita-nanda-sūnur vā
kurvāṇas taṭa-bhuvi tāṇḍavāni rāmaḥ ||33||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From dancing:

At a festival performed for Kṛṣṇa, Baladeva, surrounded by his singing friends on the bank of the Yamunā River, began to dance, moving his body about while his pearl necklace shook. His body became covered in perspiration.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
ratād, yathā śrī-daśame –
tāsām ativihāreṇa śrāntānāṃ vadanāni saḥ |
prāmṛjat karuṇaḥ premṇā śantamenāṅga pāṇinā ||34||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From amorous actions, from the Tenth Canto:

Seeing that the gopīs were fatigued from conjugal enjoyment, my dear King, merciful Kṛṣṇa lovingly wiped their faces with His comforting hand. SB 10.33.20

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (6) madaḥ –
viveka-hara ullāso madaḥ sa dvi-vidho mataḥ |
madhu-pāna-bhavo ’naṅga-vikriyā-bhara-jo ’pi ca |
gaty-aṅga-vāṇī-skhalana-dṛg-ghūrṇā-raktimādi-kṛt ||35||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Rapture which destroys all sense of discriminating power is called mada. There are two types: arising from intoxication and arising from extreme transformations due to love. In this state there is stumbling while walking, uncoordinated movement of the limbs, and uncoordinated speaking. The eyes roll, and the face becomes red.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
madhu-pāna-bhavo, yathā lalita-mādhave (5.41) –-
bile kva nu vililyire nṛpa-pipīlikāḥ pīḍitāḥ
pinasmi jagad-aṇḍakaṃ nanu hariḥ krudhaṃ dhāsyati |
śacī-gṛha-kuraṅga re hasasi kiṃ tvam ity unnadann
udeti mada-ḍambara-skhalita-cūḍam agre halī ||36||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Rapture arising from intoxication, from Lalita-mādhava:

Baladeva arrived with disheveled hair, fully intoxicated with liquor. He began to shout, “The ant-like kings, being defeated, are hiding in some hole. I will smash the whole universe. O Indra, plaything of Śacī! Why are you laughing?”

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā prācām –
bha-bha-bhramati medinī la-la-landate candramāḥ
kṛ-kṛṣṇa vavada drutaṃ ha-ha-hasanti kiṃ vṛṣṇayaḥ |
sisīdhu mu-mu-muñca me pa-pa-pa-pāna-pātre sthitaḥ
mada-skhalitam ālapan hala-dharaḥ śriyaḥ vaḥ kriyāt ||37||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example of intoxication from a traditional work:

“O Kṛṣṇa! Tell me immediately! Is the earth swerving? Is the moon wobbling? O Yadus, why are you laughing? Give me some wine in a glass!” In this way sitting in his house, Balarāma stuttered while speaking. May that Balarāma give you blessings!

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
uttamas tu madāc chete madhyo hasati pāyati |
kaniṣṭhaḥ krośati svairaṃ puruṣaṃ vakti roditi ||38||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When a person becomes intoxicated with liquor, the superior person falls asleep. The second best person laughs and sings. The lowest person shouts, uses rough words and weeps.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
mado ’pi tri-vidhaḥ proktas taruṇādi-prabhedataḥ |
atra nātyupayogitvād vistārya na hi varṇitaḥ ||39||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: There are three types of intoxication according to the stage of intoxication. However, in this work these will not be discussed as they are not very useful to the topic.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
anaṅga-vikriyā-bharajo, yathā –
vrajapati-sutam agre vikṣya bhugnībhavad-bhrūr
bhramati hasati rodity āsyam antardadhāti |
pralapati muhur ālīṃ vandate paśya vṛnde
nava-madana-madāndhā hanta gāndharvikeyam ||40||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Rapture arising from love:

O Vṛndā! See this astonishing thing! Rādhā, in the rapture of new love, gazing at Kṛṣṇa in front of her, sometimes frowns, sometimes wanders about, sometimes laughs, sometimes weeps, sometimes covers her face, sometimes prattles and sometimes repeatedly offers respects to her friends.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (7) garvaḥ –
saubhāgya-rūpa-tāruṇya-guṇa-sarvottamāśrayaiḥ |
iṣṭa-lābhādinā cānya-helanaṃ garva īryate ||41||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Treating others with contempt due to one’s own good fortune, due to youthful beauty, due to one’s good qualities, due to taking shelter of the Lord, or due to attaining one’s object of love, is called garva or haughtiness.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atra solluṇṭha-vacanaṃ līlānuttara-dāyitā |
svāṅgekṣā nihnuvo ’nyasya vacanāśravaṇādayaḥ ||42||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: In this state there are joking words, not giving answers by one’s own choice, showing off one’s body, concealing one’s intentions and not hearing others’ words.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra saubhāgyena, yathā śrī-kṛṣṇa-karṇāmṛte (3.93) –
hastam utkṣipya yāto ’si balāt kṛṣṇa kim adbhutam |
hṛdayād yadi niryāsi pauruṣaṃ gaṇayāmi te ||43||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Haughtiness arising from good fortune, from Kṛṣṇa-karṇāmṛta:

O Kṛṣṇa! Is it really astonishing if You can give up holding my hand? I will consider You a real man if You can take Yourself from my heart.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
rūpa-tāruṇyena, yathā –
yasyāḥ svabhāva-madhurāṃ pariṣevya mūrtiṃ
dhanyā babhūva nitarām api yavana-śrīḥ |
seyaṃ tvayi vraja-vadhū-śata-bhukta-mukte
dṛk-pātam ācaratu kṛṣṇa kathaṃ sakhī me ||44||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Haughtiness arising from beauty:

Endowed with the beauty of youth, my friend Rādhā is fortunate, having taken shelter of the form of natural sweetness. How can she glance at You, who have enjoyed hundreds upon hundreds of women of Vraja and then abandoned them?

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
guṇena, yathā –
gumphantu gopāḥ kusumaiḥ sugandhibhir
dāmāni kāmaṃ dhṛta-rāmaṇīyakaiḥ |
nidhāsyate kintu sa-tṛṣṇam agrataḥ
kṛṣṇo madīyāṃ hṛdi vismitaḥ srajam ||45||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Haughtiness arising from good qualities:

The cowherd boys can make unlimited garlands of the most beautiful, fragrant flowers. But Kṛṣṇa, showing great astonishment at the skill in its making, will eagerly hold over His heart my garland.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
sarvottamāśrayeṇa, yathā śrī-daśame –
tathā na te mādhava tāvakāḥ kvacid
bhraśyanti mārgāt tvayi baddha-sauhṛdāḥ
tvayābhiguptā vicaranti nirbhayā
vināyakānīkapa-mūrdhasu prabho ||46||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From taking the shelter of the Lord, from the Tenth Canto:

O Mādhava, Supreme Personality of Godhead, Lord of the goddess of fortune, if devotees completely in love with You sometimes fall from the path of devotion, they do not fall like nondevotees, for You still protect them. Thus they fearlessly traverse the heads of their opponents and continue to progress in devotional service. SB 10.2.33

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
iṣṭa-lābhena, yathā –
vṛndāvanendra bhavataḥ paramaṃ prasādam
āsādya nandita-matir muhur uddhato ’smi |
āśaṃsate muni-manoratha-vṛtti-mṛgyāṃ
vaikuṇṭha-nātha-karuṇām api nādya cetaḥ ||47||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Haughtiness from attaining one’s desired object:

O moon of Vṛndāvana! Receiving Your excellent mercy, in great bliss, I have become proud. Today my heart does not even desire the mercy of the Lord of Vaikuṇṭha which is sought by the minds of the sages.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (8) śaṅkā
svīya-cauryāparādhādeḥ para-krauryāditas tathā |
svāniṣṭotprekṣaṇaṃ yat tu sā śaṅkety abhidhīyate |
atrāsya-śoṣa-vaivarṇya-dik-prekṣā-līnatādayaḥ ||48||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Apprehension due to committing theft, offense, or others’ cruelty is called śaṅkā. In this state, there is drying of the mouth, change of complexion, glancing in all directions, and hiding oneself.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra cauryād, yathā –
sa-tarṇakaṃ ḍimbha-kadambakaṃ haran
sad-ambham ambhoruha-sambhavas tadā |
tirobhaviṣyan haritaś calekṣaṇair
aṣṭābhir aṣṭau haritaḥ samīkṣate ||49||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Apprehension from theft:

After stealing the calves and cowherd boys out of pride, Brahmā, desiring to disappear from Kṛṣṇa’s presence, out of great apprehension glanced with his eight eyes in the eight directions.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
syamantakaṃ hanta vamantam arthaṃ
nihnutya dūre yad ahaṃ prayātaḥ |
avadyam adyāpi tad eva karma
śarmāṇi citte mama nirbhinatti ||50||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

Akrura thought, “I have hidden the Syamantaka jewel which gives wealth, and fled away. In anxiety because of this despicable act, until today, happiness has disappeared from my heart.”

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
aparādhād, yathā –
tad-avadhi malino ’si nanda-goṣṭhe
yad-avadhi vṛṣṭim acīkaraḥ śacīśa |
śṛṇu hitam abhitaḥ prapadya kṛṣṇaṃ
śriyam aviśaṅkam alaṅkuru tvam aindrīm ||51||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Apprehension from offense:

O Indra! As long as you pour rain upon Nanda’s fields, you will be despondent. Listen as I tell you something for your benefit. By surrendering completely to Kṛṣṇa’s lotus feet, you will enjoy full powers as Indra without apprehension in your heart.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
para-krauryeṇa, yathā padyāvalyām (331) –
prathayati na tathā mamārtim uccaiḥ
sahacari vallava-candra-viprayogaḥ |
kaṭubhir asura-maṇḍalaiḥ parīte
danujapater nagare yathāsya vāsaḥ ||52||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Apprehension on seeing others’ cruelty, from Padyāvalī:

O friend! Thinking of Kṛṣṇa living in Mathurā surrounded by the ferocious demons of Kaṃsa, I am deeply afflicted. In the same way I feel affliction in separation from Kṛṣṇa.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration: śaṅkā tu pravara-strīṇāṃ bhīrutvād bhaya-kṛd bhavet ||53||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: This apprehension (śaṅkā) becomes fear (bhaya) in the best of women, because they have a timid nature.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (9) trāsaḥ –
trāsaḥ kṣobho hṛdi taḍid-ghora-sattvogra-nisvanaiḥ |
pārśvasthālamba-romāñca-kampa-stambha-bhramādi-kṛt ||54||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The disturbance which arises in the heart from lightning, fearful creatures or a loud sound is called trāsa (terror). In this state a person grasps nearby objects for support. His hairs stand on end. He quivers, becomes paralyzed, and wanders about.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra taḍitā, yathā –
bāḍhaṃ niviḍayā sadyas taḍitā tāḍitekṣaṇaḥ |
rakṣa kṛṣṇeti cukrośa ko ’pi gopī-stanandhayaḥ ||55||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Terror from lightning:

When the eyes of the cowherd boys became pained by the flashing of lightning, they became to shout “O Kṛṣṇa, please protect us!”

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
ghora-sattvena, yathā –
adūram āseduṣi vallavāṅganā
svaṃ puṅgavīkṛtya surāri-puṅgave |
kṛṣṇa-bhrameṇāśu taraṅgad-aṅgikā
tamālam āliṅgya babhūva niścalā ||56||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Terror from ferocious beasts:

When Vṛṣāsura, taking the form of a bull, approached, some of the gopīs began to tremble. Suddenly embracing a tamāla tree, mistaking it for Kṛṣṇa, they could not move.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
ugra-nisvanena, yathā –
ākarṇya karṇa-padavī-vipadaṃ yaśodā
visphūrjitaṃ diśi diśi prakaṭaṃ vṛkāṇām |
yāmān nikāma-caturā caturaḥ sva-putraṃ
sā netra-catvara-caraṃ ciram ācacāra ||57||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Terror arising from frightening sounds:

When the very wise Yaśodā heard the terrifying howling of wolves echoing in all directions, which gave pain to the ears, she kept Kṛṣṇa within her eyesight continually for some days.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
gātrotkampī manaḥ-kampaḥ sahasā trāsa ucyate |
pūrvāpara-vicārotthaṃ bhayaṃ trāsāt pṛthag bhavet ||58||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Disturbance of the heart which suddenly produces shaking of the limbs is called trāsa. This is different from fear. Fear arises after deliberating on previous and subsequent events.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (10) āvegaḥ –
cittasya sambhramo yaḥ syād āvego ’yaṃ sa cāṣṭadhā |
priyāpriyānala-marud-varṣotpāta-gajāritaḥ ||59||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Confusion of the mind is called āvega. It is of eight types, arising from dear things, detested objects, fire, wind, rain, calamity, elephants or enemies.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
priyotthe pulakaḥ sāntvaṃ cāpalyābhyudgamādayaḥ |
apriyotthe tu bhū-pāta-vikrośa-bhramaṇādayaḥ ||60||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: In āvega arising from dear objects, standing of hair on end, words of affection, fickleness, and rising to one’s feet appear. In āvega arising from detested objects, falling on the ground, shouting and wandering about appear.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
vyatyasta-gati-kampākṣi-mīlanāsrādayo ’gnije |
vātaje ’jāvṛti-kṣipra-gati-dṛṅ-mārjanādayaḥ ||61||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: In āvega arising from fire, the actions are retreating, shaking of the body, closing the eyes, and tears. In āvega from wind, the actions are covering the body, walking swiftly, and rubbing the eyes.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
vṛṣṭijo dhāvana-cchatra-gātra-saṅkocanādi-kṛt |
autpāte mukha-vaivarṇya-vismayo ’kaṇṭhitādayaḥ ||62||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: In āvega arising from rain, the actions are running, holding an umbrella and crouching down. In āvega arising from calamity, the actions are discoloration of the face, astonishment and strong shaking of the body.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
gāje palāyanotkampa-trāsa-pṛṣṭhekṣaṇādayaḥ |
arijo varma-śastrādi-grahāpasaraṇādikṛt ||63||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: In āvega from elephants, the actions are fleeing away, strong shaking, trāsa and looking behind. In āvega arising from enemies, the actions are taking up armor and weapons, abandoning one’s house and going elsewhere.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atra priya-darśanajo, yathā –
prekṣya vṛndāvanāt putram āyāntaṃ prasnuta-stanī |
saṅkulā pulakair āsīd ākulā gokuleśvarī ||64||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Āvega arising from seeing one’s object of affection:

When Yaśodā, the queen of Gokula, saw Kṛṣṇa returning from the forest of Vṛndāvana, her hair stood on end. She became perplexed and milk began to flow from her breast.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
priya-śravaṇajo, yathā śrī-daśame –
śrutvācyutam upāyātaṃ nityaṃ tad-darśanotsukāḥ |
tat-kathākṣipta-manaso babhūvur jāta-sambhramāḥ ||65||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Āvega arising from hearing about one’s object of affection, from the Tenth Canto:

The wives of the brāhmaṇas were always eager to see Kṛṣṇa, for their minds had been enchanted by descriptions of Him. Thus as soon as they heard that He had come, they became very excited. SB 10.23.18

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
apriya-darśanajo, yathā –
kim idaṃ kim idaṃ kim etad uccair
iti ghora-dhvani-ghūrṇitā lapantī |
niśi vakṣati vīkṣya pūtanāyās
tanayaṃ bhrāmyati sambhramād yaśodā ||66||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From seeing something detestable:

Hearing a terrifying sound and seeing Kṛṣṇa on the chest of Pūtanā during a dream at night, Yaśodā wailed in a loud voice, “What is this? What is this?” She began wandering about in confusion.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
apriya-śravaṇajo, yathā –
niśamya putraṃ kraṭatos taṭānte
mahījayor madhyagam ūrdhva-netrā |
ābhīra-rājñī hṛdi sambhrameṇa
biddhā vidheyaṃ na vidāñcakāra ||67||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Hearing something detestable:

Hearing that Kṛṣṇa was situated between two Arjuna trees on the bank of the Yamunā, Yaśodā with eyes turned upwards, became struck with confusion, and could not decide what to do.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
agnijo, yathā –
dhīr vyagrā ’jani naḥ samasta-suhṛdāṃ tvāṃ prāṇa-rakṣā-maṇiṃ
gavyā gauravataḥ samīkṣya niviḍe tiṣṭhantam antar-vane |
vahniḥ paśya śikhaṇḍa-śekhara kharaṃ muñcann akhaṇḍa-dhvaniṃ
dīrghābhiḥ sura-dīrghikāmbu-laharīm arcibhir ācāmati ||68||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Āvega arising from fire:

O Kṛṣṇa with peacock feather! See the fire making a constantly ferocious sound. It is touching the Mandākinī River of Svarga with its long flames, as if sipping its waters. You are the jewel which protects the life of Your friends. Seeing You standing in the midst of the deep forest in order to protect the cows, our hearts have become bewildered.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
vātajo, yathā –
pāṃśu-prārabdha-ketau bṛhad-aṭavi-kuṭonmāthi-śauṭīrya-puñje
bhāṇḍīroddaṇḍa-śākhā-bhuja-tatiṣu gate tāṇḍavācārya-caryām |
vāta-vrāte karīṣaṅ-kaṣatara-śikhare śārkare jhātkariṣṇau
kṣauṇyām aprekṣya putraṃ vrajapati-gṛhiṇī paśya sambambhramīti ||69||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Āvega arising from wind:

When Tṛṇāvarta, making a terrifying sound, endowed with great strength to uproot large forest trees, spreading clouds of dust, carrying cow dung, dust, grass and stones, began to sway the branches of the Bhāṇḍira tree, Yaśodā, the wife of Nanda, not seeing her son Kṛṣṇa on the ground, became overcome with great confusion.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
varṣajo, yathā śrī-daśame–
atyāsārātivātena paśavo jāta-vepanāḥ |
gopā gopyaś ca śītārtā govindaṃ śaraṇaṃ yayuḥ ||70||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Āvega arising from rain, from the Tenth Canto:

The cows and other animals, shivering from the excessive rain and wind, and the cowherd men and ladies, pained by the cold, all approached Lord Govinda for shelter. SB 10.25.11

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
samam uru-karakābhir danti-śuṇḍā-sapiṇḍāḥ
pratidiśam iha goṣṭhe vṛṣṭi-dhārāḥ patanti |
ajaniṣata yuvāno ’py ākulās tvaṃ tu bālaḥ
sphuṭam asi tad-agārān mā sma bhūr niryiyāsuḥ ||71||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

Showers of rain along with hail are falling profusely like juice from the foreheads of elephants. The young men have become confused. You are just a boy. Therefore, do not try to go out of the house.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
utpātajo, yathā –
kṣitir ativipulā ṭalaty akasmād
upari ghuranti ca hanta ghoram ulkāḥ |
mama śiśur ahi-dūṣitārka-putrī-
taṭam aṭatīty adhunā kim atra kuryām ||72||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Āvega arising from calamity:

Becoming perplexed, Yaśodā said, “The broad earth is suddenly shaking. In the sky meteors are making terrifying sounds. My young boy has just now gone to the bank of the Yamunā contaminated with the poison of Kāliya. What should I do?”

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
gājo, yathā –
apasarāpasara tvarayā gurur
mudira-sundara he purataḥ karī |
mradima-vīkṣaṇatas tava naś calaṃ
hṛdayam āvijate pura-yoṣitām ||73||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Āvega arising from elephants:

O Śyāmasundara! Flee quickly! Flee quickly! There is terrifying elephant in front of You. Because of Your sweet glances, the fickle hearts of us Mathurā women have become completely disturbed.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration: gajena duṣṭa sattvo ’ nyaḥ paśvādir upalakṣyate ||74||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: By mentioning elephant, other wicked animals such horses should be understood as well.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
caṇḍāṃśos turagān saṭāgra-naṭanair āhatya vidrāvayan
drāg andhaṅkaraṇaḥ surendra-sudṛśāṃ goṣṭhoddhūtaiḥ pāṃśubhiḥ |
pratyāsīdatu mat-puraḥ sura-ripur garvāndham arvākṛtir
drāghiṣṭhe muhur atra jāgrati bhuje vyagrāsi mātaḥ katham ||75||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

O mother! The horse demon Keśī blinds the heavenly damsels of Indra by raising the dust in the stables with his hooves. By shaking his mane, he whips the horses pulling the chariot of the sun and makes them flee away. But let that demon horse come towards Me! My long arm is ready for him! Why are you so disturbed?

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
arijo, yathā lalita-mādhave (2.29) –
sthūlas tāla-bhujān natir giritaṭī-vakṣāḥ kva yakṣādhamaḥ
kvāyaṃ bāla-tamāla-kandala-mṛduḥ kandarpa-kāntaḥ śiśuḥ |
nāsty anyaḥ saha-kāritā-paṭur iha prāṇī na jānīmahe
hā goṣṭheśvari kīdṛg adya tapasāṃ pākas tavonmīlati ||76||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Disturbance arising from enemies, from Lalita-mādhava:

Here is the lowest demon Śaṅkhacūḍa, sturdy in body, with arms long as tāla trees and chest broad as a mountain plateau. What a match for the beautiful child resembling Cupid, soft as the bud of new tamāla tree! Is there no skilful person here to help us? O queen of Vraja, I cannot understand where all the results of your austerities have gone today.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā tatraiva (5.30) –
saptiḥ saptī ratha iha rathaḥ kuñjaro me
tūṇas tūṇo dhanur uta dhanur bhoḥ kṛpāṇī kṛpāṇī |
kā bhīḥ kā bhīr ayam ayam ahaṃ hā tvaradhvaṃ tvaradhvaṃ
rājñaḥ putrī bata hṛta-hṛtā kāminā vallavena ||77||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example, also from Lalita-mādhava:

When Kṛṣṇa stole Rukmiṇī at the svayaṃvara, the kings spoke to their servants. “My horse, chariot, elephant, bow quiver, and sword are here. What fear do I have? You should be quick! The lusty cowherd has stolen the daughter of a king!”

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
āvegābhāsa evāyaṃ parāśrayatāpi cet |
nāyakotkarṣa-bodhāya tathāpy atra nidarśitaḥ ||78||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Though the above example is actually only an ābhāsa of āvega, being the sentiment of āvega in non-devotees, taking Kṛṣṇa as the enemy, because it reveals the superior nature of Kṛṣṇa, it is given as an example.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (11) unmādaḥ –
unmādo hṛd-bhramaḥ prauḍhānandāpad-virahādijaḥ ||79||
atrāṭṭa-hāso naṭanaṃ saṅgītaṃ vyartha-ceṣṭitam |
pralāpa-dhāvana-krośa-viparīta-kriyādayaḥ ||80||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Confused understanding caused by extreme bliss, calamity or separation is called unmāda (insanity).[209] In this state the actions are loud laughing, dancing, singing, useless actions, prattling, running, shouting and performing activities opposite of what are normally performed.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra prauḍhānandād, yathā karṇāmṛte (2.25) –
rādhā punātu jagad acyuta-datta-cittā
manthānakaṃ vidadhatī dadhi-rikta-pātre |
yasyāḥ stana-stavaka-cañcala-locanālir
devo ’pi ruddha-hṛdayo dhavalaṃ dudoha ||81||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Insanity arising from intense bliss, from Kṛṣṇa-karṇāmṛta:

May Rādhā who, having surrendering her heart to Kṛṣṇa, churned an empty yogurt pot, purify the world. And may Kṛṣṇa, whose eyes like bees hovered upon Rādhā’s breasts which were like flower clusters, with mind absorbed in Rādhā, and who began milking a bull, purify the world.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
āpado, yathā –
paśūn api kṛtāñjalir namati māntrikā ity alaṃ
tarūn api cikitsakā iti viṣauṣadhaṃ pṛcchati |
hradaṃ bhujaga-bhairavaṃ hari hari praviṣṭe harau
vrajendra-gṛhiṇī muhur bhrama-mayīm avasthāṃ gatā ||82||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Insanity arising from calamity:

What a calamity! When Kṛṣṇa entered the lake of Kāliya, Yaśodā became insane, and thinking the animals were knowers of mantras, folded her hands and began offering them respects. Thinking the trees were doctors, she asked them for medicine to counteract the poison.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
virahād, yathā śrī-daśame –
gāyanty uccair amum eva saṃhatā
vicikyur unmattakavad vanād vanam |
papracchur ākāśavad antaraṃ bahir
bhūteṣu santaṃ puruṣaṃ vanaspatīn ||83||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Insanity arising from separation, from the Tenth Canto:

The gopīs began to sing in a loud voice together and began to search for Kṛṣṇa in the various forests like insane persons. They began to inquire from the trees about that person who is inside and outside of all living beings like ether. SB 10.30.4

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
unmādaḥ pṛthag utko ’yaṃ vyādhiṣv antarbhavann api |
yat tatra vipralambhādau vaicitrīṃ kurute parām ||84||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Insanity could be included within sickness (vyādhiṣu). However, it is described separately because in states such as separation, it induces a unique variety of actions. Vyādhiṣu means “among different types of sickness.”

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
adhirūḍhe mahā-bhāve mohanatvam upāgate |
avasthāntaram āpto ’sau divyonmāda itīryate ||85||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When a person attains the stage of bewilderment in the adhirūḍha stage of mahā-bhāva, unmāda takes on another form called divyonmāda.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (12) apasmāraḥ –
duḥkhottha-dhātu-vaiṣamyādy-udbhūtaś citta-viplavaḥ |
apasmāro ’tra patanaṃ dhāvanāsphoṭana-bhramāḥ |
kampaḥ phena-srutir bāhu-kṣepaṇa-vikrośanādayaḥ ||86||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: A condition of almost total lack of consciousness arising from disturbance of the dhātus due to grief is called apasmāra (epilepsy). In that state there is falling on the ground, running about, pain in the limbs, confusion, shaking of the body, foaming at the mouth, flailing the arms and shouting.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
phenāyate pratipadaṃ kṣipate bhujormim
āghūrṇate luṭhati kujati līyate ca |
ambā tavādya virahe ciram amburāja-
veleva vṛṣṇi-tilaka vraja-rāja-rājñī ||87||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

O best of the Yadus! Now, our mother Yaśodā, because of pain due to separation from You for a long time is foaming at the mouth like the shore of the ocean. Her arms are moving about like waves in the ocean. She sometimes whirls about, sometimes rolls on the ground, makes sounds and sometimes remains motionless.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
śrutvā hanta hataṃ tvayā yadu-kulottaṃsātra kaṃsāsuraṃ
daityas tasya suhṛttamaḥ pariṇatiṃ ghorāṃ gataḥ kām api |
lālā-phena-kadamba-cumbita-mukha-prāntas taraṅgad-bhujo
ghūrṇann arṇava-sīmni maṇḍalatayā bhrāmyan na viśrāmyati ||88||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

Crown jewel of the Yadus! Hearing that You killed Kaṃsa, Kaṃsa’s close friends underwent unspeakable, terrible transformations. They wander on the beach whirling about like wheels and cannot stop. From their mouths, foam flows in great quantities and their arms flail about.[210]

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
unmādavad iha vyādhi-viśeṣo ’py eṣa varṇitaḥ |
parāṃ bhayānakābhāse yat karoti camatkṛtim ||89||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: This sickness called apasmāra has been described separately from sickness, as in the case of unmāda, since it produces an extremely astonishing state with a hint of bhayānaka-rasa (fear).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (13) vyādhiḥ –
doṣodreka-viyogādyair vyādhayo ye jvarādayaḥ |
iha tat-prabhavo bhāvo vyādhir ity abhidhīyate |
atra stambhaḥ ślathāṅgatva-śvāsottāpa-klamādayaḥ ||90||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Sickness such as fever generated from extreme sorrow at hearing of contempt for Kṛṣṇa by the demons or from separation or other events is called vyādhi or disease, but in this book vyādhi refers to symptoms caused by an emotional state (rather than from disturbance of the dhātus) arising from that separation. In this state, paralysis, slackness of the limbs, heavy breathing, anxiety and fatigue occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā
tava cira-viraheṇa prāpya pīḍām idānīṃ
dadhad-uru-jaḍimāni dhmāpitāny aṅgakāni |
śvasita-pavana-dhāṭī-ghaṭṭita-ghrāṇa-vāṭaṃ
luṭhati dharaṇi-pṛṣṭhe goṣṭha-vāṭī-kuṭumbam ||91||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: O Kṛṣṇa! Separated from You for a long time, Your associates in Vraja are afflicted. Their bodies are burning and remain motionless. Their nostrils quiver because of heavy breathing, and they roll on the ground.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (14) mohaḥ –
moho hṛn-mūḍhatā harṣād viśleṣād bhayatas tathā |
viṣādādeś ca tatra syād dehasya patanaṃ bhuvi |
śūnyendriyatvaṃ bhramaṇaṃ tathā niśceṣṭatā-mayaḥ ||92||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: A complete lack of awareness (internal inaction) arising from joy, separation, fear or lamentation is called moha. In this state there is falling on the ground, absence of sense perceptions, wandering around and inactivity.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra harṣād, yathā śrī-daśame
itthaṃ sma pṛṣṭaḥ sa tu bādarāyaṇis
tat-smāritānanta-hṛtākhilendriyaḥ |
kṛcchrāt punar labdha-bahir-dṛśiḥ śanaiḥ
pratyāha taṃ bhāg avatottamottamam ||93||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Moha arising from joy, from the Tenth Canto:

O Śaunaka, greatest of saints and devotees, when Mahārāja Parīkṣit inquired from Śukadeva Gosvāmī in this way, Śukadeva Gosvāmī, immediately remembering subject matters about Kṛṣṇa within the core of his heart, externally lost contact with the actions of his senses. Thereafter, with great difficulty, he revived his external sensory perception and began to speak to Mahārāja Parīkṣit about kṛṣṇa-kathā. SB 10.12.44

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
nirucchvasita-rītayo vighaṭitākṣipa-kṣma-kriyā
nirīha-nikhilendriyāḥ pratinivṛtta-cid-vṛttayaḥ |
avekṣya kuru-maṇḍale rahasi puṇḍarīkekṣaṇaṃ
vrajāmbuja-dṛśo ’bhajan kanaka-śālabhañjī-śriyam ||94||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example of moha arising from joy:

Seeing Kṛṣṇa alone in Kurukṣetra, the women of Vraja stopped breathing, stopped blinking their eyes, stopped all actions and became devoid of consciousness. They remained standing there like golden statues.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
viśleṣād, yathā haṃsadūte (4) –
kadācit khedāgniṃ vighaṭayitum antar-gatam asau
sahālībhir lebhe taralita-manā yāmuna-taṭīm |
cirād asyāś cittaṃ paricita-kuṭīra-kalanād
avasthā tastāra sphuṭam atha suṣupteḥ priya-sakhī ||95||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Moha arising from separation, from Haṃsadūta:

Once, Rādhā, in order to assuage the fire of separation in her heart, went to the bank of the Yamunā with her friends, but seeing there the familiar bower of creepers, her heart became covered with a blank state of mind--which was her dear friend, like deep sleep.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
bhayād, yathā –
mukundam āviṣkṛta-viśva-rūpaṃ
nirūpayan vānara-varya-ketuḥ |
karāravindāt purataḥ skhalantaṃ
na gāṇḍīvaṃ khaṇḍita-dhīr viveda ||96||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Moha arising from fear:

When Kṛṣṇa showed His universal form, Arjuna, who had the insignia of Hanumān on his flag, seeing that form, dropped his Gāṇḍiva bow. However, being in a state of moha, he was not aware of this.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
viṣādād, yathā śrī-daśame–
kṛṣṇaṃ mahā-baka-grastaṃ dṛṣṭvā rāmādayo ’rbhakāḥ |
babhūvur indriyāṇīva vinā prāṇaṃ vicetasaḥ ||97||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Moha arising from despair, from the Tenth Canto:

When Balarāma and the other boys saw that Kṛṣṇa had been devoured by the gigantic duck, they became unconscious, like senses without life. SB 10.11.49

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
asyānyatrātma-paryante syāt sarvatraiva mūḍhatā |
kṛṣṇa-sphūrti-viśeṣas tu na kadāpy atra līyate ||98||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: When the devotees develop moha, they lose awareness of objects including their own bodies, but awareness of Kṛṣṇa never disappears.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (15) mṛtiḥ –
viṣāda-vyādhi-santrāsa-samprahāra-klamādibhiḥ |
prāṇa-tyāgo mṛtis tasyām avyaktākṣara-bhāṣaṇam |
vivarṇa-gātratā-śvāsa-māndya-hikkādayaḥ kriyāḥ ||99||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Giving up life because of grief, sickness, fear, beating or exhaustion is called mṛti (death). In this state, unclear speaking, change of bodily color, feeble breathing and hiccups occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
anullāsa-śvāsā muhur asaralottānita-dṛśo
vivṛṇvantaḥ kāye kim api nava-vaivarṇyam abhitaḥ |
harer nāmāvyaktīkṛtam alaghu-hikkā-laharībhiḥ
prajalpantaḥ prāṇān jahati mathurāyāṃ sukṛtinaḥ ||100||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The pious persons of Mathurā, with weak breathing, eyes glancing sideways and upwards, taking on a unique complexion in their bodies and hiccupping loudly, gave up their lives while uttering Kṛṣṇa’s name unclearly.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
viramad-alaghu-kaṇṭhodghoṣa-ghutkāra-cakrā
kṣaṇa-vighaṭita-tāmyad-dṛṣṭi-khadyota-dīptiḥ |
hari-mihira-nipīta-prāṇa-gāḍhāndhakārā
kṣayam agamad akasmāt pūtanā kāla-rātriḥ ||101||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

The sun in the form of Kṛṣṇa drank up the deep darkness of life of the midnight in the form of Pūtanā. Her eyes lit up for a moment beyond her control and then died out, like the glowing of fireflies in the night. That midnight suddenly disappeared with the loud hooting of owls in the form of her death groans.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
prāyo ’tra maraṇāt pūrvā citta-vṛttir mṛtir matā |
mṛtir atrānubhāvaḥ syād iti kenacid ucyate |
kintu nāyaka-vīry ārthaṃ śatrau maraṇam ucyate ||102||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The state of consciousness just prior to death is generally called mṛti. However, some say that mṛti should be considered only an external similarity to death (anubhāva). It has been described in the enemies of Kṛṣṇa (though it is not the vyabhicāri-bhāva called mṛti) just in order to show His power.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (16) ālasyam –
sāmarthyasyāpi sad-bhāve kriyānunmukhatā hi yā |
tṛpti-śramādi-sambhūtā tad-ālasyam udīryate ||103||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Lack of enthusiasm to do activities because of satiation or fatigue, even though one has the ability to do them is called ālasya.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atrāṅga-bhaṅgo jṛmbhā ca kriyā dveṣo ’kṣi-mardanam |
śayyāsanaika-priyatā tandrā-nidrādayo ’pi ca ||104||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: In this state, stretching the limbs, yawning, disgust with work, rubbing the eyes, lying down, fondness for sitting down, exhaustion and sleep occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra tṛpter, yathā –
viprāṇāṃ nas tathā tṛptir āsīd govardhanotsave |
nāśīrvāde ’pi gopendra yathā syāt prabhaviṣṇutā ||105||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
ālasya arising from satiation:

O king of the cowherds! At the festival of Govardhana we have become so satisfied that we cannot even give blessings.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
śramād, yathā –
suṣṭhu niḥsaha-tanuḥ subalo ’bhūt
prītaye mama vidhāya niyuddham |
moṭayantam abhito nijam aṅgaṃ
nāhavāya sahasāhvayatām amum ||106||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
ālasya arising from fatigue:

After arm wrestling with me to please me, he cannot do any activity now and he is stretching his limbs. You should not call him to fight immediately.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (17) jāḍyam –
jāḍyam apratipattiḥ syād iṣṭāniṣṭha-śrutīkṣaṇaiḥ |
virahādyaiś ca tan-mohāt pūrvāvasthāparāpi ca |
atrānimiṣatā tūṣṇīm-bhāva-vismaraṇādayaḥ ||107||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Absence of the ability to decide anything, which arises from hearing or seeing desirable or undesirable things or which arises from separation, is called jāḍyam. This occurs previous to and following moha (inoperative mind). In this state, blinking of the eyes, silence and forgetfulness occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra iṣṭa-śrutyā, yathā śrī-daśame–
gāvaś ca kṛṣṇamukha-nirgata-veṇu-gīta-
pīyūṣam uttabhita-karṇa-puṭaiḥ pibantyaḥ |
śāvāḥ snuta-stana-payaḥ-kavalāḥ sma tasthur
govindam ātmani dṛśāśru-kulāḥ spṛśantyaḥ ||108||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From hearing what is desirable, from the Tenth Canto:

Using their upraised ears as vessels, the cows are drinking the nectar of the flute-song flowing out of Kṛṣṇa’s mouth. The calves, their mouths full of milk from their mothers’ moist nipples, stand still as they take Govinda within themselves through their tear-filled eyes and embrace Him within their hearts. SB 10.21.14

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
aniṣṭa-śrutyā, yathā –
ākalayya parivartita-gotrāṃ
keśavasya giram arpita-śalyām |
biddha-dhīr adhika-nirnimiṣākṣī-
lakṣaṇā kṣaṇam avartata tūṣṇīm ||109||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From hearing what is undesirable:

Hearing Keśava call out someone else’s name, the heart of Lakṣmaṇā, one of the leaders of the gopīs, was pained. Her eyes stopped blinking and she did not utter a word.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
iṣṭekṣaṇena, yathā śrī-daśame–
govindaṃ gṛham ānīya deva-deveśam ādṛtaḥ |
pūjāyāṃ nāvidat kṛtyaṃ pramādopahato nṛpaḥ ||110||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From seeing the object of desire, from the Tenth Canto:

King Yudhiṣṭhira respectfully brought Lord Govinda, the Supreme God of gods, to his personal quarters. The King was so overcome with joy that he could not remember all the rituals of worship. SB 10.71.40

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
aniṣṭekṣaṇena, yathā tatraiva
yāvad ālakṣyate ketur yāvad reṇū rathasya ca |
anuprasthāpitātmāno lekhyānīvopalakṣitāḥ ||111||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From seeing the undesirable, from the Tenth Canto:

Sending their minds after Kṛṣṇa, the gopīs stood as motionless as figures in a painting. They remained there as long as the flag atop the chariot was visible, and even until they could no longer see the dust raised by the chariot wheels. SB 10.39.37

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
viraheṇa, yathā –
mukunda viraheṇa te vidhuritāḥ sakhāyaś cirād
alaṅkṛtibhir ujjhitā bhuvi niviśya tatra sthitāḥ |
skhalan-malina-vāsasaḥ śavala-rukṣa-gātra-śriyaḥ
sphuranti khala-devala-dvija-gṛhe surārcā iva ||112||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From separation:

Your friends, pained by long separation from You, remain on this earth like the deities of neglectful brāhmaṇas which are without ornaments, wearing soiled cloth which is falling off, with limbs dirty and thin.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (18) vrīḍā –
navīna-saṅgamākāryas tavāvajñādinā kṛtā |
adhṛṣṭatā bhaved vrīḍā tatra maunaṃ vicintanam |
avaguṇṭhana-bhū-lekhau tathādhomukhatādayaḥ ||113||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The state of bashfulness, the opposite of audacity, arising from just meeting one’s lover, from performing forbidden actions, from praise or neglect is called vrīḍā (shyness). In this state there is silence, anxiety, covering the head, writing on the ground, and hanging the head.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra navīna-saṅgamena, yathā padyāvalyām (198) –
govinde svayam akaroḥ saroja-netre
premāndhā vara-vapur arpaṇaṃ sakhi |
kārpaṇyaṃ na kuru darāvaloka-dāne
vikrīte kariṇi kim aṅkuśe vivādaḥ ||114||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From meeting the Lord for the first time, from Padyāvalī:

O lotus-eyed friend! Blinded with love, you have offered your beautiful body to Govinda. O friend! Do not be miserly by showing yourself to Him only a little. The purchased elephant does not quarrel with the goad.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
akāryeṇa, yathā –
tvam avāg iha mā śiraḥ kṛthā
vadanaṃ ca trapayā śacī-pate |
naya kalpa-taruṃ na cec chacīṃ
katham agre mukham īkṣayiṣyasi ||115||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Shame arising from doing forbidden actions:

O Indra! You should not hang down your head in shame and remain silent. Take the parijāta tree and go. Otherwise how can you show your face to your wife?

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
stavena, yathā –
bhūri-sādguṇya-bhāreṇa stūyamānasya śauriṇā |
uddhavasya vyarociṣṭa namrī-bhūtaṃ tadā śiraḥ ||116||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Shame from being praised:

When Kṛṣṇa praised Uddhava, listing all of his good qualities, Uddhava lowered his head and took on a unique attractiveness.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
avajñayā, yathā hari-vaṃśe (2.67.19) satyādevī-vākyam –
vasanta-kusumaiś citraṃ sadā raivatakaṃ girim |
priyā bhūtvā ’priyā bhūtā kathaṃ drakṣyāmi taṃ punaḥ ||117||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Shame arising from neglect, from Hari-vaṃśa, in a statement by Satyā:

Raivataka Mountain is always glorious with spring flowers. How can I look upon that mountain when I have lost the affection of Kṛṣṇa, though once I most dear to Him?

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (19) avahitthā –
avahitthākāra-guptir bhaved bhāvena kenacit ||118||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The external action of wanting to hide one’s external symptoms because of thinking oneself low is called avahitthā.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atrāṅgādeḥ parābhyūha-sthānasya parigūhanam |
anyatrekṣā vṛthā-ceṣṭā vāg-bhaṅgīty-ādayaḥ kriyāḥ ||119||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: In this state, hiding one’s limbs so others will think one is something else, glancing elsewhere, useless actions, and clever use of words occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tathā coktam –
anubhāva-pidhānārtho ’vahitthaṃ bhāva ucyate ||120||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The ancient authorities say that the bhāva which conceals ones anubhāvas (external symptoms) is called the avahitthā-vyabhicāri bhāva.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra jaihmyena, yathā śrī-daśame –
sabhājayitvā tam anaṅga-dīpanaṃ
sahāsa-līlekṣaṇa-vibhrama-bhruvā |
saṃsparśanenāṅka-kṛtāṅghri-hastayoḥ
saṃstutya īṣat kupitā babhāṣire ||121||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Out of deceit:

Śrī Kṛṣṇa had awakened romantic desires within the gopīs, and they honored Him by glancing at Him with playful smiles, gesturing amorously with their eyebrows, and massaging His hands and feet as they held them in their laps. Even while worshiping Him, however, they felt somewhat angry (which they concealed), and thus they spoke to Him. SB 10.32.16

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
dākṣiṇyena, yathā –
sātrājitī-sadana-sīmani pārijāte
nīte praṇīta-mahasā madhusūdanena |
drāghīya-sīmani vidarbha-bhuvas taderṣyāṃ
sauśīlyataḥ kila na ko ’pi vidām babhūva ||122||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Out of mild nature:

When Madhusūdana brought the parijāta tree to the house of Satyabhāmā, though Rukmiṇī was filled with anger, no one could detect that deception because of her mildness.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
hriyā, yathā prathame–
tam ātmajair dṛṣṭibhir antarātmanā
duranta-bhāvāḥ parirebhire patim |
niruddham apy āsravad ambu netrayor
vilajjatīnāṃ bhṛgu-varya vaiklavāt ||123||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Concealment out of bashfulness, from the First Canto:

The insuperable ecstasy was so strong that the queens, who were shy, first embraced the Lord in the innermost recesses of their hearts. Then they embraced Him visually, and then they sent their sons to embrace Him [which is equal to personal embracing]. But, O chief amongst the Bhṛgus, though they tried to restrain their feelings, they inadvertently shed tears. SB 1.11.32

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
jaihmya-hrībhyāṃ, yathā –
kā vṛṣasyati taṃ goṣṭha-bhujaṅgaṃ kula-pālikā |
dūti yatra smṛte mūrtir bhītyā romāñcitā mama ||124||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From deceit and bashfulness:

O messenger! Will a respectable women desire such a snake among cowherds? Remembering Him, the hairs of my body are standing on end out of fear.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
saujanyena, yathā –
gūḍhā gābhīrya-sampadbhir mano-gahvara-garbhagā |
prauḍhāpy asyā ratiḥ kṛṣṇe durvitarkā parair abhūt ||125||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Concealment by good qualities:

Though Rādhā’s attachment to Kṛṣṇa increased to the extreme, by the wealth of her self-control, she concealed it so that no one could doubt her.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
gauraveṇa, yathā –
govinde subala-mukhaiḥ samaṃ suhṛdbhiḥ
smerāsyaiḥ sphuṭam iha narma nirmimāṇe |
ānamrīkṛta-vadanaḥ pramoda-mugdho
yatnena smitam atha saṃvavāra patrī ||126||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Concealment out of respect:

When Kṛṣṇa began to joke among His laughing cowherd friends, His servant Patrī became jubilant. Out of respect, he hung his head and with great difficulty covered his laughing.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
hetuḥ kaścid bhavet kaścid gopyaḥ kaścana gopanaḥ |
iti bhāva-trayasyātra viniyogaḥ samīkṣyate ||127||
hetutvaṃ gopanatvaṃ ca gopyatvaṃ cātra sambhavet |
prāyeṇa sarva-bhāvānām ekaśo ’nekaśo’pi ca ||128||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Three bhāvas will be seen operating in this situation: one as a cause, one which is concealed and one bhāva which conceals the other. Individually or in a group, the bhāvas may act as a cause, being concealed or concealing.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (20) smṛtiḥ –
yā syāt pūrvānubhūtārtha-pratītiḥ sadṛśekṣayā |
dṛḍhyābhyāsādinā vāpi sā smṛtiḥ parikīrtitā |
bhaved atra śiraḥ-kampo bhrū-vikṣepādayo ’pi ca ||129||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Scrutiny of previous experience, which arises from strict practice or from seeing similar objects, is called smṛti (remembrance). In this state shaking the head and moving the brows occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra sadṛśekṣaṇā, yathā –
vilokya śyāmam ambhodam ambhoruha-vilocanā |
smāraṃ smāraṃ mukunda tvāṃ smāraṃ vikramam anvabhūt ||130||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Smṛti arising from seeing a similar object:

O Mukunda! When lotus-eyed Rādhā saw a dark cloud she remembered You and experienced the force of love.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
dṛḍhābhyāsena, yathā –
praṇidhāna-vidhim idānīm akurvato ’pi pramādato hṛdi me |
hari-pada-paṅkaja-yugalaṃ kvacit kadācit parisphurati ||131||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Smṛti arising from determined practice:

Without concentrating, spontaneously, the lotus feet of the Lord now appear in my heart at any time and at any place.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (21) vitarkaḥ –
vimarṣāt saṃśayādeś ca vitarkas tūha ucyate |
eṣa bhrū-ksepaṇa-śiro ’ṅguli-sañcālanādi-kṛt ||132||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Arriving at a conclusion based on error, doubt or inference is called vitarka (conjecture). In this state, moving the brows and moving the head and fingers occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra vimarṣād, yathā vidagdha-mādhave (2.27) –
na jānīṣe mūrdhnaś cyutam api śikhaṇḍaṃ yad akhilaṃ
na kaṇṭhe yan mālyaṃ kalayasi purastāt kṛtam api |
tad unnītaṃ vṛndāvana-kuhara-līlā-kalabha he
sphuṭaṃ rādhā-netra-bhramara-vara vīryonnatir iyam ||133||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Vitarka arising from inference, from Vidagdha-mādhava:

O elephant who sports in the houses of Vṛndāvana! The peacock feather has fallen from Your head on the ground, but You are unaware of that. There is a prepared garland lying in front of You, but You do not put it on. From that I can infer that the power of the bees, in the form of Rādhā’s eyes, has agitated You.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
saṃśayāt, yathā –
asau kiṃ tāpiñcho na hi tad-amala-śrīr iha gatiḥ
payodaḥ kiṃ vāmaṃ na yad iha niraṅgo himakaraḥ |
jagan-mohārambhoddhūra-madhura-vaṃśī-dhvanir ito
dhruvaṃ mūrdhany adrer vidhumukhi mukundo viharati ||134||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Vitarka arising from doubt:

Is that a tamāla tree? It cannot be, for why would it endowed with such pure, clear movements? Is it a cloud? No, it cannot be, for a spotless moon is residing there. O moonfaced one! It seems that Mukunda, who can enchant the universe with the sound of His flute, is certainly wandering on the top of Govardhana Mountain.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration: vinirṇayānta evāyaṃ tarka ity ūcire pare ||135||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Some say that tarka means to draw conclusions about objects capable of being judged.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (22) cintā –
dhyānaṃ cintā bhaved iṣṭānāpty-aniṣṭāpti-nirmitam |
śvāsādhomukha-bhūlekha-vaivarṇyān nidratā iha |
vilāpottāpa-kṛśatā-bāṣpa-dainyādayo ’pi ca ||136||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Pondering, arising from not attaining a desired object or from attaining an undesirable object, is called cintā. In this state, heavy breathing, hanging the head, writing on the ground, change of color, sleeplessness, prattle, and fever occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra iṣṭānāptyā, yathā śrī-daśame
kṛtvā mukhāny avaśucaḥ śvasanena śuṣyad
bimbādharāṇi caraṇena likhantyaḥ |
asrer upāttamasibhiḥ kucakuṅkumāni
tasthur mṛjantya uruduḥkha-bharāḥ sma tūṣṇīm ||137||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Pondering arising from not attaining the desired object of love, from the Tenth Canto:

Their heads hanging down and their heavy, sorrowful breathing drying up their reddened lips, the gopīs scratched the ground with their toes. Tears flowed from their eyes, carrying their kajjala and washing away the vermilion smeared on their breasts. Thus they stood, silently bearing the burden of their unhappiness. SB 10.29.29

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā –
aratibhir atikramya kṣāmā pradoṣam adoṣadhīḥ
katham api cirād adhyāsīnā praghāṇam aghāntaka |
vidhūrita-mukhī ghūrṇaty antaḥ prasūs tava cintayā
kim ahaha gṛhaṃ krīḍā-lubdha tvayādya visasmare ||138||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

O killer of Agha! Your affectionate mother, depressed and thin from thinking of You, remained sitting on the veranda from a long time, and having waited till evening, she now wanders about within the house. How astonishing it is! Though You experienced such fun there, You have completely forgotten about Your house.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
aniṣṭāptyā, yathā –
gṛhiṇi gahanayāntaścintayonnidra-netrā
glapaya na mukha-padmaṃ tapta-bāṣpa-plavena |
nṛpa-puram anuvindan gāndineyena sārdhaṃ
tava sutam aham eva drāk parāvartayāmi ||139||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Cintā arising from attaining something undesirable:

Do not remain sleepless, absorbed in intense deliberation, with hot tears withering your lotus face. I will go to Mathurā with Akrura and bring back your son very soon.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (23) matiḥ –
śāstrādīnāṃ vicārottham artha-nirdhāraṇaṃ matiḥ ||140||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Ascertaining a meaning after consulting scripture is called mati.[211]

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atra kartavya-karaṇaṃ saṃśaya-bhramayoś chidā |
upadeśaś ca śiṣyāṇām ūhāpohādayo ’pi ca ||141||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: In this state performing necessary actions after cutting doubts and illusions, giving instructions to students, and defeating other’s arguments and opposite conclusions occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā pādme vaiśākha-māhātmye –
vyāmohāya carācarasya jagatas te te purāṇāgamās
tāṃ tām eva hi devatāṃ paramikāṃ jalpantu kalpāvadhi |
siddhānte punar eka eva bhagavān viṣṇuḥ samastāgama-
vyāpāreṣu vivecana-vyatikaraṃ nīteṣu niścīyate ||142||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From the Padma Purāṇa, Vaiśākha-maḥātmya:

Let the purāṇas and other scriptures glorify here and there for a kalpa the greatness of their respective devatās in order to produce illusion in the people of this world. But after taking account of all varieties of interpretation, their conclusion is that Viṣṇu alone is the Supreme Lord.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā śrī-daśame–
tvaṃ nyasta-daṇḍamunibhir gaditānubhāva
ātmātmadaś ca jagatām iti me vṛto ’si |
hitvā bhavad-bhruva udīrita-kāla-vega-
dhvastāśiṣo ’bja-bhavanākapatīn kuto ’nye ||143||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From the Tenth Canto:

Knowing that great sages who have renounced all attachments proclaim Your glories, that You are the Supreme Soul of all the worlds, and that You are so gracious that You give away even Your own self, I chose You as my husband, rejecting Lord Brahmā, Lord Śiva and the rulers of heaven, whose aspirations are all frustrated by the force of time, which is born from Your eyebrows. What interest, then, could I have in any other suitors? SB 10.60.39

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (24) dhṛtiḥ –
dhṛtiḥ syāt pūrṇatā jñāna-duḥkhābhāvottamāptibhiḥ |
aprāptātīta-naṣṭārthān abhisaṃśocanādi-kṛt ||144||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The steadiness of heart arising from attaining realization of the Lord, from absence of suffering in attaining realization of the Lord, and from realizing prema with the Lord is called dhṛti. In this state there is no lamentation for things not attained or for things which have disappeared.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra jñānena, yathā vairāgya-śatake (55) bhartṛhariḥ –
aśnīmahi vayaṃ bhikṣām āśāvāso vasīmahi |
śayīmahi mahī-pṛṣṭhe kurvīmahi kim īśvaraiḥ ||145||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From attaining realization of the Lord, from Bhartṛhari’s Vairāgya-śataka:

When I attain knowledge related to the Lord, I will eat only begged food and live without clothing. I will sleep on the ground. What is the necessity of serving the king or other authorities?

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
duḥkhābhāvena, yathā –
goṣṭhaṃ ramā-keli-gṛhaṃ cakāsti
gāvaś ca dhāvanti paraḥ-parārdhāḥ |
putras tathā dīvyati divya-karmā
tṛptir mamābhūd gṛhamedhi-saukhye ||146||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From lack of suffering:

Our cow sheds have become the playground of Lakṣmī and more than a 100,000 billion cows are running around. A divine child is playing in the house. I am fully satisfied with the happiness of family life.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
uttamāptyā, yathā –
harilīlā-sudhā-sindhos taṭam apy adhitiṣṭhataḥ |
mano mama caturvargaṃ tṛṇāyāpi na manyate ||147||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From attaining prema:

I am situated on the bank of the ocean of nectar consisting of the Lord’s pastimes. Thus my mind is not aware of artha, dharma, kāma and mokṣa, which are now worthless like grass.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (25) harṣaḥ –
abhīṣṭekṣaṇa-lābhādi-jātā cetaḥ-prasannatā |
harṣaḥ syād iha romāñcaḥ svedo ’śru mukha-phullatā |
āvegonmāda-jaḍatās tathā mohādayo ’pi ca ||148||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Happiness of the heart arising from seeing or attaining one’s desired object is called harṣa. In this state standing of hair on end, perspiration, tears, glowing face, confusion (āvega), insanity (unmāda), indecision jaḍatā) and fainting (moha) occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra abhīṣṭekṣaṇena, yathā śrī-viṣṇu-purāṇe [ViP 5.17.25]
tau dṛṣṭvā vikasad-vaktra-sarojaḥ sa mahāmatiḥ |
pulakāñcita-sarvāṅgas tadākrūro ’bhavan mune ||149||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Joy on seeing one’s desired object, from Viṣṇu Purāṇa:

O sage! When Akrura saw Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, his lotus-faced blossomed with joy and all his hairs stood on end.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
abhīṣṭa-lābhena, yathā śrī-daśame
tatraikāṃsagataṃ bāhuṃ kṛṣṇasyotpalasaurabham |
candanāliptam āghrāya hṛṣṭaromā cucumba ha ||150||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From attaining one’s desired object, from the Tenth Canto:

Upon the shoulder of one gopī Kṛṣṇa placed His arm, whose natural blue-lotus fragrance was mixed with that of the sandalwood pulp anointing it. As the gopī relished that fragrance, her bodily hair stood on end in jubilation, and she kissed His arm. SB 10.33.11

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (26) autsukyam –
kālākṣamatvam autsukyam iṣṭekṣāpti-spṛhādibhiḥ |
mukha-śoṣa-tvarā-cintā-niḥśvāsa-sthiratādikṛt ||151||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Inability to tolerate the passing of time, arising from desire to see or attain a desired object is called autsukyam (impatience). In this state there is drying of the mouth, haste, pondering and prominence of breathing.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra iṣṭekṣā-spṛhayā, yathā śrī-daśame –
prāptaṃ niśamya nara-locana-pāna-pātram
autsukya-viślathita-keśa-dukūla-baddhāḥ |
sadyo visṛjya gṛha-karma patīṃś ca talpe
draṣṭuṃ yayur yuvatayaḥ sma narendra-mārge ||152||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Impatience arising from a desire to see one’s cherished object, from the Tenth Canto:

When the young women of the city heard that Lord Kṛṣṇa, the reservoir of pleasure for human eyes, had arrived, they hurriedly went onto the royal road to see Him. They abandoned their household duties and even left their husbands in bed, and in their eagerness the knots of their hair and garments came loose. SB 10.71.34

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā, stavāvalyāṃ śrī-rādhikāṣṭake (14.7) –
prakaṭita-nija-vāsaṃ snigdha-veṇu-praṇādair
druta-gati harim ārāt prāpya kuñje smitākṣī |
śravaṇa-kuhara-kaṇḍuṃ tanvatī namra-vaktrā
snapayati nija-dāsye rādhikā māṃ kadā nu ||153||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From Stavāvalī, Śrī-rādhikāṣṭaka:

When Kṛṣṇa revealed his whereabouts in the grove by the sound of His flute, Rādhā, coming quickly to the grove with smiling face, remained waiting with head hung down, eager to hear Kṛṣṇa’s words. When will that Rādhā engage me in her service?

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
iṣṭāpti-spṛhayā, yathā –
narma-karmaṭhatayā sakhī-gaṇe
drāghayaty aghaharāgrataḥ kathām |
gucchaka-grahaṇa-kaitavād asau
gahvaraṃ druta-pada-kramaṃ yayau ||154||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Impatience arising from a desire to attain one’s desired object:

When the gopīs tried to prolong the conversation with Kṛṣṇa by expert joking, and thus delay Him, Rādhā came to the grove quickly, on the pretext of accepting a bunch of flowers.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (27) augryam –
aparādha-durukty-ādi- jātaṃ caṇḍatvam ugratā |
vadha-bandha-śiraḥ-kampa-bhartsanottāḍanādi-kṛt ||155||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Ferocity arising from offenses and harsh words is called augrya. In that state killing, binding, shaking the head, shouting loudly and beating occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra aparādhād, yathā –
sphurati mayi bhujaṅgī-garbha-viśraṃsi-kīrtau
viracayati mad-īśe kilbiṣaṃ kāliyo ’pi |
huta-bhuji bata kuryāṃ jāṭhare vauṣaḍ enaṃ
sapadi danuja-hantuḥ kintu roṣād bibhemi ||156||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Augrya arising from offense (to Kṛṣṇa):

Garuḍa speaks: By my power the snakes have abortions. But in my presence, Kāliya is offending my master. I want to offer him to the fire in my stomach, but I am afraid of Kṛṣṇa’s anger.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
duruktito, yathā sahadevoktiḥ –
prabhavati vibudhānām agrimasyāgra-pūjāṃ
na hi danuja-ripor yaḥ prauṅdha-kīrter visoḍhum |
kaṭutara-yama-daṇḍoddaṇḍa-rocir mayāsau
śirasi pṛthuni tasya nyasyate savya-pādaḥ ||157||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Augrya arising from harsh words (against Kṛṣṇa), in a statement by Sahadeva:

Sahadeva speaks: I will place with more force than the punishment of Yama my left foot on the head of that person who cannot tolerate the first worship of Kṛṣṇa--who is full of all glories and worth of worship by all devatās.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā baladevoktiḥ –
ratāḥ kila nṛpāsane kṣitipa-lakṣa-bhuktojjhite
khalāḥ kuru-kulādhamāḥ prabhum ajāṇḍa-koṭiṣv amī |
hahā bata viḍambanā śiva śivādya naḥ śṛṇvatāṃ
haṭhād iha kaṭākṣayanty akhila-vandyam apy acyutam ||158||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Baladeva speaks:

O Lord! These evil men, lowest members of Kuru dynasty, having attained and given up the qualities of kings, are attached to sitting on the king’s throne. How painful it is to have to hear them today in the assembly boldly insulting Acyuta, worthy of praise by the whole universe.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (28) amarṣaḥ –
adhikṣepāpamānādeḥ syād amarṣo ’sahiṣṇutā ||159||
tatra svedaḥ śiraḥkampo vivarṇatvaṃ vicintanam |
upāyānveṣaṇākrośa-vaimukhyottāḍanādayaḥ ||160||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Intolerance arising from contempt or insult or other causes is called amarṣa (indignation). In this state perspiration, shaking the head, change of color, pondering, looking for methods, shouting, turning away and beating occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra adhikṣepād, yathā vidagdha-mādhave (2.53) –
nirdhautānām akhila-dharaṇī-mādhurīṇām dhūrīnām
kalyāṇī me nivasati vadhūḥ paśya pārśve navoḍhā |
antargoṣṭhe caṭula naṭayann atra netra-tribhāgaṃ
niḥśaṅkas tvaṃ bhramasi bhavitā nākulatvaṃ kuto me ||161||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Indignation arising from contempt, from Vidagdha-mādhava:

Jaṭilā said to Kṛṣṇa: See! My son’s new, auspicious bride, endowed with all the sweetness of the earth, is sitting by my side. O unsteady boy! You cannot disturb me, though You wander fearlessly through Vraja moving Your eyebrows!

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
apamānād, yathā padmoktiḥ –
kadamba-vana-taskara drutam apehi kiṃ cāṭubhir
jane bhavati mad-vidhe paribhavo hi nātaḥ paraḥ |
tvayā vraja-mṛgī-dṛśāṃ sadasi hanta candrāvalī
varāpi yad ayogyayā sphuṭam adūṣi tārākhyayā ||162||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Indignation arising from disrespect, in the words of Padmā:

O thief in the kadamba forest! Quickly come here and do not use clever words. There is no greater disrespect for a person like me than directly to spoil excellent Candrāvalī in the assembly of gopīs by uttering the unsuitable name of Rādhā.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
ādi-śabdād vañcanād api, yathā śrī-daśame –
pati-sutānvaya-bhārtṛ-bāndhavān
ativilaṅghya te ’nty acyutāgatāḥ |
gati-vidas tavodgīta-mohitāḥ
kitava yoṣitaḥ kas tyajen niśi ||163||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Indignation arising from being cheated, indicated by the word ādi:

Dear Acyuta, You know very well why we have come here. Who but a cheater like You would abandon young women who come to see Him in the middle of the night, enchanted by the loud song of His flute? Just to see You, we have completely rejected our husbands, children, ancestors, brothers and other relatives. SB 10.31.17

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (29) asūyā –
dveṣaḥ parodaye ’sūyānya-saubhāgya-guṇādibhiḥ |
tatrerṣyānādarākṣepā doṣāropo guṇeṣv api |
apavṛttis tiro-vīkṣā bhruvor bhaṅguratādayaḥ ||164||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Hatred arising from the others’ increase of good fortune or qualities is called asūyā (envy or fault-finding). In this state, malice, disrespect, insult, fault finding, speaking ill of others, casting evil glances and moving the eyebrows occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra anya-saubhāgyena, yathā padyāvalyām (302)–
mā garvam udvaha kapola-tale cakāsti
kṛṣṇa-svahasta-likhitā nava-mañjarīti |
anyāpi kiṃ na sakhi bhājanam īdṛśīnāṃ
vairī na ced bhavati vepathur antarāyaḥ ||165||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Envy arising from others’ increase of good fortune, from Padyāvalī:

Do not be proud, now that you attain the glory of a new mañjarī marked with the hand of Kṛṣṇa on your forehead. Can no one else be the recipient of that mark? Others would also have this good fortune if our enemy’s hand did not shake.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā vā śrī-daśame–
tasyā amūni naḥ kṣobhaṃ kurvanty uccaiḥ padāni yat |
yaikāpahṛtya gopīnāṃ raho bhuṅkte ’cyutādharam ||166||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
From the Tenth Canto:

These footprints of that special gopī greatly disturb us. Of all the gopīs, she alone was taken away to a secluded place, where she is enjoying the lips of Kṛṣṇa. SB 10.30.31

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
guṇena, yathā –
svayaṃ parājayaṃ prāptān kṛṣṇa-pakṣān vijitya naḥ |
baliṣṭhā bala-pakṣāś ced durbalāḥ ke tataḥ kṣitau ||167||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Envy arising from increase of good qualities:

Balarāma’s team thinks itself strong and able to defeat our team with Kṛṣṇa on our side, but is there anyone weaker than Balarāma’s team in this world?

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (30) cāpalyam –
rāga-dveṣādibhiś citta-lāghavaṃ cāpalaṃ bhavet |
tatrāvicāra-pāruṣya-svacchandācaraṇādayaḥ ||168||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Cāpalam (insolence) means inconsiderateness of the heart arising from attraction or repulsion. In this state, lack of judgment, rough words and careless actions occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra rāgeṇa, yathā śrī-daśame–
śvo bhāvini tvam ajitodvahane vidarbhān
guptaḥ sametya pṛtanā-patibhiḥ parītaḥ |
nirmathya caidya-magadheśa-balaṃ prasahya
māṃ rākṣasena vidhinodvaha vīrya-śulkām ||169||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Cāpalya arising from attachment:

O unconquerable one, tomorrow when my marriage ceremony is about to begin, You should arrive unseen in Vidarbha and surround Yourself with the leaders of Your army. Then crush the forces of Caidya and Magadhendra and marry me in the Rākṣasa style, winning me with Your valor. SB 10.52.41

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
dveṣeṇa, yathā –
vaṃśī pūreṇa kālindyāḥ sindhuṃ vindatu vāhitā |
guror api puro nīvīṃ yā bhraṃśayati subhruvām ||170||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Cāpalya arising from hatred:

May the flute, which loosens the cords tying the clothing of the beautiful women, enter the ocean on the waves of the Yamunā!

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (31) nidrā –
cintālasya-nisarga-klamādibhiś citta-mīlanaṃ nidrā |
tatrāṅga-bhaṅga-jṛmbhā-jāḍya-śvāsākṣi-mīlanāni syuḥ ||171||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Suspension of external awareness arising from pondering, lack of energy, natural tendency, and fatigue is called nidrā or sleep. In this state, rubbing the limbs, yawning, inaction, heavy breathing, and closing the eyes occur.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra cintayā, yathā –
lohitāyati mārtaṇḍe veṇu-dhvanim aśṛṇvatī |
cintayākrānta-hṛdayā nidadrau nanda-gehinī ||172||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Nidrā arising from worry:

When the sun turned red at sunset and she did not hear the sound of the flute, Yaśodā entered a state of nidrā, being afflicted by excessive pondering the situation.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
ālasyena, yathā –
dāmodarasya bandhana-karmabhir atiniḥsahāṅga-latikeyam |
dara-vighūrṇitottamāṅgā kṛtāṅga-bhaṅgā vrajeśvarī sphurati ||173||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Nidrā arising from lack of energy:

When Yaśodā tied up Kṛṣṇa, she was unable to move her body. She became dizzy. Rubbing her limbs, she entered a state of nidrā.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
nisargeṇa, yathā –
aghahara tava vīrya-proṣitāśeṣa-cintāḥ
parihṛta-gṛha-vāstu-dvāra-bandhānubaddhāḥ |
nija-nijam iha rātrau prāṅganaṃ śobhayantaḥ
sukham avicalad-aṅgāḥ śerate paśya gopāḥ ||174||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Nidrā arising from natural urge:

O killer of Agha! Look! Having extinguished all fears by thinking of Your exploits, the cowherd men have given up locking their doors and sleep at night in the yards of their houses without moving their limbs.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
klamena, yathā –
saṅkrānta-dhātu-citrā suratānte sā nitānta-tāntā ’dya |
vakṣasi nikṣiptāṅgī harer viśākhā yayau nidrām ||175||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Nidrā arising from fatigue:

Viśākhā, tinged with the colors from Kṛṣṇa’s cosmetics, tired after enjoyment, is sleeping on the chest of Kṛṣṇa.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yuktāsya sphūrti-mātreṇa nirviśeṣeṇa kenacit |
hṛn-mīlanāt puro ’vasthā nidrā bhakteṣu kathyate ||176||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The state just previous to extinguishing of consciousness, in which there is the appearance of Kṛṣṇa without particular pastimes, is called nidrā for the devotees.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (32) suptiḥ –
suptir nidrā-vibhāvā syān nānārthānubhavātmikā |
indriyoparati-śvāsa-netra-saṃmīlanādi-kṛt ||177||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Sleep in which there are various thoughts and an experience of objects is called supti or dreaming. In this state there is absence of the functioning of the external senses, heavy breathing and closing the eyes.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
kāmaṃ tāmarasākṣa keli-vitatiḥ prāduṣkṛtā śaiśavī
darpaḥ sarpa-pates tad asya tarasā nirdhūyatām uddhūraḥ |
ity utsvapna-girā cirād yadu-sabhāṃ vismāpayan smerayan
niḥśvāsena darottaraṅgad-udaraṃ nidrāṃ gato lāṅgalī ||178||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
An example:

Baladeva astonished the assembly of Yadus and made them laugh, when he went to sleep. While breathing heavily and heaving his belly, he exclaimed in a dream state, “O lotus-eyed Kṛṣṇa! You have magnificently manifested your wealth of childhood pastimes, by powerfully crushing the intolerable pride of Kāliya, king of the snakes.”

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
atha (33) bodhaḥ –
avidyā-moha-nidrāder dhvaṃsodbodhaḥ prabuddhatā ||179||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Enlightenment or appearance of knowledge caused by destruction of ignorance, destruction of moha and destruction of sleep is called bodha.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra avidyā-dhvaṃsataḥ –
avidyā-dhvaṃsato bodho vidyodaya-puraḥsaraḥ |
aśeṣa-kleśa-viśrānti-svarūpāvagamādi-kṛt ||180||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Bodha arising from destruction of ignorance:

Enlightenment occurs after the appearance of knowledge which occurs after removal of ignorance. This enlightenment consists of realization one’s identity with brahman, which destroys unlimited suffering.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
yathā –
vindan vidyā-dīpikāṃ sva-svarūpaṃ
buddhvā sadyaḥ satya-vijñāna-rūpam |
niṣpratyūhas tat paraṃ brahma mūrtaṃ
sāndrānandākāram anveṣayāmi ||181||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Another example:

Realizing my svarūpa of eternity and knowledge after attaining the lamp of knowledge, without obstacle, I will now search for the supreme brahman personified, composed of concentrated bliss.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
moha-dhvaṃsataḥ –
bodho moha-kṣayāc chabda-gandha-sparśa-rasair hareḥ |
dṛg-unmīlana-romāñca-dharotthānādi-kṛd bhavet ||182||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Bodha arising from destruction of moha:

When moha is destroyed by the sound, fragrance, touch and taste of the Lord, there is bodha. In this state the eyes are open, hairs stand on end, and there is rising from the ground.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra śabdena, yathā –
prathama-darśana-rūḍha-sukhāvalī-
kavalitendriya-vṛttir abhūd iyam |
agha-bhidaḥ kila nāmny udite śrutau
lalitayodamimīlad ihākṣiṇī ||183||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Bodha from destruction of moha, arising from sound of the Lord:

All of Rādhā’s sense stopped functioning (moha) due to the bliss generated from first seeing Kṛṣṇa. Then when Lalitā uttered the names of Kṛṣṇa in her ear, she opened her eyes.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
gandhena, yathā –
aciram agha-hareṇa tyāgataḥ srasta-gātrī
vana-bhuvi śavalāṅgī śānta-niḥśvāsa-vṛttiḥ |
prasarati vana-mālā saurabhe paśya rādhā
pulakita-tanur eṣā pāṃśu-puñjād udasthāt ||184||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Arising from smell:

When Kṛṣṇa once disappeared from the presence of Rādhā, she lost control of her limbs, lost her color, and lost her breath. She fell on the forest earth. When the fragrance of Kṛṣṇa’s forest garland spread in all directions, Rādhā’s hairs stood on end by smelling the fragrance, and look--she rose from the earth.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
sparśena, yathā –
asau pāṇi-sparśo madhura-masṛṇaḥ kasya vijayī
viśīryantyāḥ saura-pulina-vanam ālokya mama yaḥ |
durantām uddhūya prasabham abhito vaiśasa-mayīṃ
drutaṃ mūrcchām antaḥ sakhi sukha-mayīṃ pallavayati ||185||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Breaking moha by touch:

O friend! Whose touch is this, which is soft, blissful and all-conquering? Seeing the bank of the Yamunā I had fainted. The touch of that hand completely removed with force my fainting condition which gave me so much suffering, and produced in me a fainting condition of happiness.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
rasena, yathā –
antarhite tvayi balānuja rāsa-kelau
srastāṅga-yaṣṭir ajaniṣṭa sakhī visaṃjñā |
tāmbūla-carvitam avāpya tavāmbujākṣī
nyastaṃ mayā mukha-puṭe pulakojjvalāsīt ||186||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Destruction of moha caused by taste:

Younger brother of Balarāma! When You disappeared during the rāsa dance, my friend Rādhā lost control of her body and became unconscious. But when lotus-eyed Rādhā tasted Your chewed tāmbūla which I placed in her mouth, her hairs stood on end.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
nidrādhvaṃsataḥ –
bodho nidrākṣayāt svapna-nidrā-pūrti-svanādibhiḥ |
tatrākṣi-mardanaṃ śayyā-mokṣo ’ṅga-valanādayaḥ ||187||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Bodha arising from breaking sleep:

There is bodha when sleep is broken by a dream, by sufficient sleep and by noise. In this state rubbing the eyes, rising from bed and rubbing the limbs occurs.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tatra svapnena, yathā –
iyaṃ te hāsa-śrīr viramatu vimuñcāñcalam idaṃ
na yāvad-vṛddhāyai sphuṭam abhidadhe tvac-caṭulatām |
iti svapne jalpanty aciram avabuddhā gurum asau
puro dṛṣṭvā gaurī namita-mukha-bimbā muhur abhūt ||188||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Bodha arising from breaking sleep through a dream:

“O Kṛṣṇa! Do not laugh. Stop pulling the edge of my cloth, otherwise I will tell Jaṭilā about Your fickle behavior.” Saying this in a dream, Rādhā suddenly woke up. Seeing the elders in front of her, she became very bashful and hung her head.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
nidrā-pūrtyā, yathā –
dūtī cāgāt tad-āgāraṃ jajāgāra ca rādhikā |
tūrṇaṃ puṇyavatīnāṃ hi tanoti phalam udyamaḥ ||189||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
Bodha arising from breaking sleep due to sufficient sleep:

Just when the messenger arrived at her house, Rādhā woke up. It is seen that the attempts of those with pious credits quickly bear fruit.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
svanena, yathā –
dūrād vidrāvayan nidrā-marālīr gopa-subhruvām |
sāraṅga-raṅgadaṃ reje veṇu-vārida-garjitam ||190||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation:
By sound:

Just as the rumbling of the clouds which gives joy to the peacocks causes the swans to fly away, so the sound of the flute broke the sleep of the gopīs.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
iti bhāvās trayas-triṃśat kathitā vyabhicāriṇaḥ |
śreṣṭha-madhya-kaniṣṭheṣu varṇanīyā yathocitam ||191||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Thus the thirty-three vyabhicāri-bhāvas have been described. They should be described as superior, moderate and inferior according to their condition.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
mātsaryodvega-dambherṣyā viveko nirṇayas tathā |
klaibyaṃ kṣamā ca kutukam utkaṇṭhā vinayo ’pi ca ||192||
saṃśayo dhārṣṭyam ity ādyā bhāvā ye syuḥ pare ’pi ca |
ukteṣv antarbhavantīti na pṛthaktvena darśitāḥ ||193||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: All other conditions such as envy, agitation, deceit, spite, discrimination, coming to conclusion, impotence, toleration, curiosity, longing, modesty, doubt, and audacity can be included in the thirty-three and are thus not described separately

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
tathā hi –
asūyāyāṃ tu mātsaryaṃ trāse ’py udvega eva tu |
dambhas tathāvahitthāyām īrṣyāmarṣe matāv ubhau |
viveko nirṇayaś cemau dainye klaibyaṃ kṣamā dhṛtau ||194||
autsukye kutukotkaṇṭhe lajjāyāṃ vinayas tathā |
saṃśayo’ntarbhavet tarke tathā dhārṣṭyaṃ ca cāpale ||195||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Thus mātsarya (envy) is included in asūyā. Udvega (agitation) is included in trāsa. Dambha (deceit) is included in avahitthā. Īrṣyā (spite) is included in amarṣa. Viveka (discrimination) and nirṇaya (concluding) are included in mati. Klaibyam (impotence) is included in dainyam. Kṣamā (toleration) is included in dhṛti. Kutuka (curiosity) and utkaṇṭha (longing) are included in autsukya. Vinaya (modesty) is included in lajjā. Saṃśaya (doubt) is included in vitarka. Dhārṣṭya (audacity) is included in cāpala.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
eṣāṃ sañcāri-bhāvānāṃ madhye kaścana kasyacit |
vibhāvaś cānubhāvaś ca bhaved eva parasparam ||196||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Among the vyabhicāri-bhāvas some act as cause (vibhāva) and others act as effect (anubhāva).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
nirvede tu yatherṣyāyā bhaved atra vibhāvatā |
asūyāyāṃ punas tasyā vyaktam uktānubhāvatā ||197||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Thus īrṣyā (malice) is the cause of nirveda (self-disgust) and the effect of asūyā (envy). This has already been stated.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
autsukyaṃ prati cintāyāḥ kathitātrānubhāvatā |
nidrāṃ prati vibhāvatvam evaṃ jñeyaḥ pare ’py amī ||198||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Cintā (pondering) is the effect of autsukya (impatience) and the cause of nidrā (sleep). In this way one should understand how the vyabhicāri-bhāvas act mutually as anubhāva (actions as effect) and vibhāva (actions as cause).[212]

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
eṣāṃ ca sāttvikānāṃ ca tathā nānā-kriyā-tateḥ |
kārya-kāraṇa-bhāvas tu jñeyaḥ prāyeṇa lokataḥ ||199||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: The causes and effects of vyabhicāri-bhāvas, sāttvika-bhāvas and various other actions should be understood to be similar to situations in the material world.

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
nindāyās tu vibhāvatvaṃ vaivarṇyāmarṣayor matam |
asūyāyāṃ punas tasyāḥ kathitaivānubhāvatā ||200||
bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.translation: Criticism or other actions are considered the cause of change of color (sāttvika-bhāva) and amarṣa (indignation, vyabhicāri-bhāva), and the effect of asūyā (envy, vyabhicāri-bhāva).

bhakti_rasamrta_sindhu.transliteration:
prahārasya vibhāvatvaṃ saṃmoha-pralayau prati |
augryaṃ pratyanubhāvatvam evaṃ jñeyāḥ pare ’pi ca ||201||
